# Check Codex concurrency capacity

## User
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T18:31:18.471Z

Perform a non-modifying concurrency-capacity check for this Codex session.

Read the repository-root `AGENTS.md`, `PLANS.md`, and `.codex/config.toml`, then determine:

1. The configured `agents.max_threads`.
2. The configured `agents.max_depth`.
3. The total number of agent slots currently exposed by the Codex host.
4. Whether the coordinator counts toward that total.
5. The maximum number of direct worker subagents that can run simultaneously right now.
6. Whether the available capacity differs from the configured maximum.
7. Whether repository trust or another setting is limiting capacity.

Where safely possible, verify the result empirically by launching the maximum number of harmless, distinct, read-only workers that each return only their lane number and completion status. Do not inspect application code, edit files, run tests, start servers, or modify Git state.

Terminate all test workers after verification and report the configured limit, actual host limit, coordinator usage, and practical simultaneous worker count.



## Assistant
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T18:31:27.047Z

I’ll keep this strictly read-only. I’m first checking the governing files and session configuration, then I’ll probe the host with numbered no-op workers and cleanly stop any that remain active.

Never create duplicate prompts, duplicate full-repository scans without different purposes, microscopic artificial lanes, overlapping writers, or workers spawned only to reach twelve.\n\nWhen a substantial task has materially more ready independent work than active workers, either fill safe capacity or record the concrete reason for under-utilization. \"The coordinator can do it,\" sequential simplicity, prior low worker count, planning effort, or deferring ready work are not sufficient reasons. Unavailable thread capacity, overlapping writes, strict dependency, an exclusive runtime/database/migration/lockfile/generated artifact/browser state, undefined scope, destructive operations, security/authorization boundaries, or high duplication risk are valid reasons.\n\n## Progressive fan-out\n\nVerify the minimum safety baseline first: repository root, applicable instructions, user scope, working-tree state, existing task state, current modifications, exclusive resources, and destructive boundaries. Former feature branches are integrated history. The development-only `/dev/animations` route is not an obsolete application.\n- Generated, local, archived, or excluded locations include `node_modules`, `.next`, `out`, `build`, `coverage`, `.runtime`, `.data`, `backups`, local database files, `test-results`, `playwright-report`, `playwright/.auth`, `artifacts/screenshots`, `artifacts/validation`, local logs, and Codex import/cache/temp content identified by `.gitignore`. Playwright normally owns an isolated server on port `3100`, and full validation normally uses port `3200` for production restart proof. Those are controlled validation resources, not authorization to start competing development servers. An explicitly isolated alternate development port is an exception for a demonstrated need, not a new application copy.\n\n## Common parallelization triggers\n\nThe following commonly satisfy the mandatory two-lane threshold:\n\n- multiple packages, services, subsystems, routes, pages, feature domains, or acceptance criteria;\n- repository-wide audits, large codebase discovery, independent bug hypotheses, or multiple evidence sources;\n- multiple animation libraries, renderers, or independent review categories;\n- separable frontend, backend, database, API, deployment, documentation, test, accessibility, security, performance, functional, or maintainability concerns;\n- broad refactors or implementations with non-overlapping ownership boundaries;\n- independent test suites or validation surfaces; or\n- noisy exploration that would displace requirements and integration state from the coordinator's context.\n\nBroad read-heavy work strongly favors parallel specialist lanes.\n\n## Work that normally remains serialized\n\nKeep work sequential when delegation overhead exceeds the work or when ordering or exclusive ownership is material, including:\n\n- tiny localized tasks;\n- strictly dependent steps;\n- changes concentrated in the same small group of files;\n- ordered migration chains;\n- package-manager, manifest, or lockfile updates with one authoritative writer;\n- shared schema, configuration, entrypoint, route registry, global style, or central provider changes;\n- destructive operations, production deployment, final integration, and final merges;\n- one exclusive runtime, browser, database, or deployment resource; or\n- any situation where parallel writes would create a race or ambiguous ownership.\n\nFor a substantial task performed primarily sequentially, record the concrete dependency, safety, resource, or conflict reason. Coordinator convenience alone is not sufficient.\n\n## Coordinator responsibilities\n\nThe main agent is the coordinator. It must:\n\n- retain the complete user request and governing constraints;\n- perform or own repository/Git preflight, decompose the dependency graph, assign non-overlapping scopes, and prevent duplicate work;\n- give every lane a bounded objective, authorization, ownership boundary, evidence requirement, validation requirement, completion condition, and return format;\n- track dependencies, lane progress, available capacity, and shared resources while keeping raw logs and noisy exploration out of the main context;\n- start replacement or newly ready lanes as soon as safe capacity becomes available;\n- collect concise evidence-backed summaries, reconcile disagreements, and directly verify severe, risky, or uncertain findings;\n- own shared-file integration, integrate in dependency order, review the combined diff, run final cross-cutting validation, and reread the original request before completion;\n- produce one coherent final result rather than concatenating worker reports; and\n- own the single mandatory `Codex_Chats` / `Development_Docs` finalization workflow unless it explicitly delegates that exclusive responsibility.\n\nThe coordinator's primary role is orchestration, requirements retention, decision-making, conflict resolution, integration, and final verification. It must not remain a general-purpose worker while safe meaningful work remains undelegated. Do not redo delegated work except for necessary verification. Workers must not fetch, pull, rebase, merge, commit, push, or run the synchronization workflow unless the coordinator explicitly grants that bounded authority.\n\n## Subagent contract\n\nEvery delegated lane must state:\n\n- lane ID, role, precise objective, relevant background, and governing requirements;\n- explicit scope and non-scope;\n- read-only or write authorization;\n- owned files, directories, components, or concerns, plus forbidden paths and resources;\n- dependencies, prerequisite evidence, and shared resources it may not modify;\n- required searches, output, evidence, focused validation, and confidence level;\n- concise return schema covering what was inspected and found, paths/symbols, evidence, authorized changes, tests, failures, uncertainty, conflicts, recommendations, and completion status;\n- completion and stop conditions; and\n- instructions to report blockers, overlaps, partial results, uncertainty, and ownership conflicts honestly.\n\nSubagents must not silently broaden scope. They return distilled findings or implementation summaries with relevant paths, symbols, commands, reproduction evidence, and confidence, not unfiltered command logs.\n\n## Capacity and wave scheduling\n\n- Treat the configured thread cap as an upper bound, not proof that every client currently exposes that many slots. Reserve the root thread for coordination when it counts toward the cap.\n- Launch every ready independent lane up to actual safe capacity. Queue excess lanes by dependency, criticality, uncertainty, and value.\n- When a slot opens, start the next ready lane immediately; do not wait for the rest of a wave.\n- Reuse coordinator discovery and a shared repository census. Bound each lane to its own evidence or ownership area instead of making every worker rediscover the entire repository.\n- Avoid redundant searches, duplicated expensive operations, idle workers, and artificial lanes created only to raise agent count.\n- Add lanes dynamically when new independent work appears. Collapse or cancel redundant lanes, preserve useful partial results, and never ignore a slower worker's material output merely because faster lanes finished first.\n\n## Worker capability and reasoning routing\n\nWhen the installed client supports per-agent selection, verify available models and reasoning settings rather than inventing aliases. Use the fastest capable available worker, generally with balanced reasoning, for mechanical repository census, file/symbol/route/import inventories, dependency mapping, repetitive documentation checks, straightforward result classification, and homogeneous read-only audits. Use a stronger worker with high reasoning for complex code paths, performance, accessibility, security, architecture, data flow, difficult implementation, subtle regressions, runtime diagnosis, and cross-system contracts. Use the strongest appropriate available capability for coordination, decomposition, reconciliation, high-risk decisions, shared architecture, integration, synthesis, and final verification. Never downgrade a lane when doing so would materially reduce reliability, and do not run every mechanical lane at the coordinator's maximum reasoning effort.\n\n## Read-heavy workflow policy\n\nAudits, reviews, discovery, research, mapping, inventories, diagnostics, and verification strongly default to parallel read-only specialist lanes divided by subsystem, concern, library, package, route group, risk category, evidence source, or acceptance criterion. Use repository-wide search before deep file inspection, reuse a shared repository census, and give every lane a distinct evidence boundary. A verification lane or the coordinator must recheck severe or disputed findings. Reports distinguish confirmed findings, probable findings, and unverified suspicions and include paths, symbols, reproduction steps, evidence, and confidence where applicable.\n\nRead-only lanes may inspect a shared file concurrently but may not mutate Git state, runtime state, databases, generated output, or external systems without explicit authorization.\n\nBroad read-heavy tasks should normally use more workers than write-heavy tasks because read-only concurrency presents fewer collision risks.\n\n## Write-heavy workflow policy\n\n- Partition implementation by non-overlapping files, components, packages, or services. Exactly one agent may write a file at a time.\n- Use separate Codex worktrees for independent implementation chats or lanes when supported and appropriate. Otherwise declare strict path ownership before editing.\n- Designate one integration owner for shared files. Lockfiles, manifests, central configuration, schemas, migrations, generated artifacts, global styles, providers, route definitions, and entrypoints each have one authoritative lane.\n- Sequence dependent changes through the plan's dependency graph. Use an explicit handoff before ownership moves between agents.\n- Each implementation lane runs focused checks for its bounded work; centralized integration validation follows combination.\n- Never casually merge over, overwrite, stage, or discard another lane's or the user's changes.\n\n## Runtime and expensive-resource ownership\n\nAssign one authoritative owner for each shared or expensive resource: the development server, port `3000`, shared browser sessions, databases, migration execution, dependency installation, manifests and lockfiles, production builds, full end-to-end suites, full validation, generated assets, deployment environments, final Git integration, `Codex_Chats` synchronization, and `Development_Docs` synchronization.\n\nOther lanes may consume existing logs or results and request targeted checks from the owner. They must not start a competing server, mutate shared database state, regenerate shared artifacts, install dependencies, or run a duplicate full suite without a demonstrated need and safe isolation. Before acquiring a runtime, inspect recorded state and listeners; release or hand off ownership explicitly.\n\n## Testing and validation parallelization\n\n- Run safe independent unit-test groups concurrently and shard by package, subsystem, feature, or test type.\n- Let implementation lanes run focused checks for their work. Do not make every lane run the complete suite.\n- Assign one integrated type-check owner, one integrated lint owner when needed, one production-build owner, one final E2E owner, and one final full-validation owner. A single validation lane may own several of these serialized operations.\n- Run the full required acceptance gate once on the ready integrated state unless the task or failure investigation requires an additional run.\n- Preserve failure evidence and classify failures as pre-existing, task-induced, environmental, intermittent, or unresolved.\n- Treat shared-database mutation suites and runtime-dependent checks as exclusive resources unless isolation is proven.\n\n## Context management\n\nThe main thread retains requirements, decisions, dependency state, ownership, integration status, unresolved questions, and final conclusions. Subagents absorb noisy searches, logs, stack traces, and repetitive evidence gathering, then return compact structured summaries. Store or reference large raw output instead of repeatedly pasting it. Summarization must not drop user requirements, unknowns, decisions, blockers, or unreviewed results.\n\n## Failure and recovery\n\nIf one lane fails, independent lanes continue. The failed lane returns partial evidence and its blocker and safely releases any file, runtime, or resource lock. Preserve completed work and useful output. Retry only when conditions change; otherwise narrow, split, reassign, or serialize only the affected portion. Do not restart all work because one lane failed. Record downstream impact, disclose unresolved gaps, and request user intervention only when authority, information, or an external state change is genuinely required.\n\n## Completion standard\n\nDo not claim a nontrivial task complete until:\n\n- every required workstream is complete or explicitly accounted for;\n- all agent output that could materially change the result has been reviewed;\n- duplicates, contradictions, and conflicting conclusions are reconciled or disclosed;\n- shared-file integration and ownership handoffs are complete;\n- focused and required cross-cutting validation has run on the integrated state;\n- the result has been rechecked against the original request;\n- skipped checks, known limitations, unresolved risks, and pre-existing failures are reported accurately;\n- final repository synchronization has run exactly once under the authorized coordinator; and\n- no worker remains active whose output could materially change the conclusion.\n\n## Execution plans\n\nEvery task uses the Tier 0 parallelization minimum in root `PLANS.md`. Standard multi-step work uses Tier 1, and every substantial cross-system, audit, refactor, migration, documentation, architecture, long-running, or high-risk implementation uses Tier 2. The plan may remain in task coordination state or be written to the requested artifact. When a durable authored plan is required, place it under `Development_Docs`; root `PLANS.md` is the reusable standard, not a competing plan archive. Update plans when discoveries change dependencies, ownership, safe concurrency, validation, or risk, while preserving completed work.\n\n## Shared development documentation\n\n`Development_Docs` at the repository root is the canonical shared location for development requirements, project history, architecture, decisions, implementation plans, and related authored documentation.\n\nBefore beginning work that depends on those materials:\n\n1. Confirm the repository, active branch, upstream, and complete working-tree state.\n2. Preserve any local modifications, then run `git fetch --prune origin`.\n3. Determine whether the branch is behind, ahead, or diverged with `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\n4. Pull with `--ff-only`, or rebase when the repository's branch policy permits it, only when the operation is demonstrably safe. Never discard local work to synchronize.\n5. If the same document changed on two computers, preserve both sets of work and surface conflicts. Perform a normal textual merge only when clearly correct. Keep clearly differentiated variants of unmergeable binary documents when appropriate, and never push unresolved conflict markers.\n\nNever use `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, `git checkout -- .`, `git restore .`, `git push --force`, or `git push --force-with-lease` as synchronization shortcuts.\n\nInspect `Development_Docs` before work when project history, requirements, architecture, decisions, or plans are relevant. Update a relevant document when an implementation decision makes it inaccurate, but do not modify unrelated documents merely because they were read. Preserve authored content and native formatting, avoid normalization churn, and do not use the directory for routine terminal output or generated test/build artifacts. Never upload secrets, credentials, private configuration, production data, or unapproved sensitive company information.\n\n## Mandatory task finalization\n\nFor every Codex task performed in this repository, conversation and development-document synchronization is a required finalization gate. Do not delete or weaken these steps:\n\n1. Complete and test the requested project work.\n2. Inspect the complete working tree and classify task changes, `Development_Docs` changes, `Codex_Chats` changes, unrelated pre-existing work, and generated files. Never stage the whole repository by default.\n3. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --dry-run`. Review conversation classifications and the structured `development_docs` status, including eligible, excluded, suspicious, large, conflicted, renamed, and deleted paths.\n4. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py` to ingest every currently accessible project-associated Codex session and any newer export in `Codex_Chats/imports/`, and to commit eligible `Development_Docs` changes through the same path-scoped workflow.\n5. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --validate`, inspect the exact synchronization-owned Git diff, and re-check the complete repository status.\n6. Confirm each command's exit status and structured report. Never claim a conversation was archived unless a source was read and a transcript was written successfully. Never claim documentation was synchronized unless the reported commit and push state prove it.\n7. If synchronization content changed, confirm the scoped commit and permitted push succeeded and verify the remote SHA. If nothing changed, confirm no empty commit was created. Report unrelated remaining changes accurately.\n8. Include concise `Chat archive:` and `Development docs:` result lines in the final response.\n\nThe synchronizer is deliberately not a `post-commit` hook because many prompts finish without a code commit. The Codex host currently exposes no repository task-completion hook that can be versioned here, so this instruction is authoritative. A task's final assistant message can only be captured on the next synchronization because it does not exist until after the pre-finalization run; the next task must update that same stable conversation rather than creating a duplicate.\n\n`Development_Docs` remains a normal Git-tracked directory. Do not copy it into `Codex_Chats`, archive it as transcripts, or maintain a duplicate mirror. The synchronizer may commit only explicit eligible paths; it must leave ignored, suspicious, oversized, conflicted, and unrelated files untouched. Do not commit raw ChatGPT exports or bypass a secret warning, unsafe Git state, ambiguous classification, source failure, integrity failure, branch divergence, or protected-branch policy.\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

It is a reusable execution standard, not a plan for one feature or audit. Root `AGENTS.md` supplies the governing behavior, requires this file at the thresholds below, and remains the automatically discovered repository instruction source. When a task requires a durable authored plan or implementation record, store that task-specific artifact under `Development_Docs` in accordance with its rules. A plan may otherwise remain in the task's coordination state or in the user-requested artifact.\n\n## Progressive planning tiers\n\nEvery task gets a parallelization assessment, but planning ceremony scales with task size and risk.\n\n### Tier 0: localized task\n\nUse Tier 0 only when the task is genuinely small, scope is narrow, one file or a tightly related file group is involved, no meaningful independent workstream exists, no shared runtime/database/migration/lockfile/deployment risk exists, and delegation would cost more than it saves.\n\nTier 0 records only the objective, affected scope, quick parallelization assessment, focused validation, and finalization. A distinct verification lane may still be useful.\n\n### Tier 1: standard multi-step task\n\nUse Tier 1 for medium work. Record the objective; scope and exclusions; governing sources; repository/working-tree reality; assumptions and unknowns; compact dependency graph and parallelization assessment; lane matrix; file and shared-resource ownership; focused testing; integration; and final verification. Use two to five workers when that many meaningful lanes exist.\n\n### Tier 2: large or high-risk task\n\nUse the complete template in this file for repository-wide audits, large implementations, major refactors, multiple feature surfaces, database changes, migrations, deployment, high-risk security work, architecture changes, long-running or cross-computer work, tasks with six or more meaningful lanes, or work with substantial context/coordination-loss risk. Target six to twelve direct workers when the dependency graph and installed capacity support that many real lanes.\n\n## Progressive planning rule\n\nMinimum safe preflight comes first. Launch obvious independent read-only lanes immediately afterward and refine the detailed plan while they run. No write lane begins before file/resource ownership and integration boundaries are defined. Plans are living coordination artifacts, not paperwork gates: make them detailed enough to protect quality without delaying safe useful work. \"Complete every planning table before starting any worker\" is prohibited when ready read-only lanes exist.\n\n## Planning principles\n\nAn execution plan must:\n\n- be self-contained enough for another coordinator to continue without rediscovering the task;\n- preserve the exact user goal, terminology, constraints, acceptance criteria, methodology, and output format;\n- separate requirements and invariants from implementation choices;\n- identify verified facts, assumptions, unknowns, and canonical sources;\n- represent work as a dependency graph rather than a falsely linear checklist;\n- maximize safe, useful concurrency while identifying conflict-prone and serialized work;\n- establish one coordinator, clear lane ownership, and one writer per file at a time;\n- identify shared runtime and expensive resources with authoritative owners;\n- define evidence, focused validation, integration validation, and completion criteria before implementation;\n- remain updateable as discoveries change scope, dependencies, ownership, risk, or validation;\n- record significant decisions and discoveries without exposing private chain-of-thought; and\n- preserve completed work when the plan changes.\n\n## Capacity and wave scheduling\n\nRecord:\n\n```text\nConfigured open-thread capacity:\nCoordinator reservation:\nMaximum normal direct workers:\nCurrent ready lanes:\nCurrently active lanes:\nBlocked lanes:\nAvailable slots:\nNext ready lanes:\nExclusive resources currently held:\n```\n\nKeep safe capacity filled with ready meaningful work, reserve the coordinator when the client counts the root session, queue excess work by dependency and value, and fill newly available slots immediately. Do not wait for an entire wave to finish. Avoid idle or artificial workers, stop or collapse redundant work, and preserve results between waves.\n\n## Lane-decomposition quality check\n\nBefore spawning a worker, confirm:\n\n- the lane has a distinct result and evidence or ownership boundary;\n- it can proceed without another lane's unfinished result;\n- it can run without conflicting writes;\n- any exclusive resource and its owner are explicit;\n- its output materially helps the final result and does not duplicate another lane;\n- the selected worker is capable of completing it independently;\n- the return format is clear; and\n- the coordinator can integrate the result.\n\nMerge or serialize lanes that fail this check.\n\n## Complete Tier 2 execution-plan template\n\nTier 2 uses every section below. Tier 1 uses the identified compact subset; Tier 0 uses its stated minimum. \"Not applicable\" is acceptable only with a short reason.\n\n### 1. Task identity\n\n```text\nTask title:\nDate:\nCoordinator:\nRepository and root:\nCurrent branch/worktree and commit:\nGoverning documents:\nRelated issue, phase, audit, or feature:\nCurrent task instructions:\nPlan location/status:\n```\n\nCopy or faithfully summarize the controlling task instructions. Link to the full source when a summary could lose detail.\n\n### 2. Objective\n\nRecord:\n\n- desired end state;\n- user-visible outcome;\n- technical outcome; and\n- why the work is being done.\n\nUse outcome language. Do not describe activity alone as success.\n\n### 3. Scope\n\nRecord:\n\n- included work;\n- explicit exclusions;\n- areas and behaviors that must remain unchanged;\n- existing completed or uncommitted work that must be preserved; and\n- actions that require separate user authority.\n\nParallelization never broadens this scope.\n\n### 4. Sources of truth\n\nList, in priority order:\n\n- current user instructions;\n- governing specifications and approved `Development_Docs` material;\n- maintained architecture, security, testing, and operational documentation;\n- canonical code and data directories;\n- relevant tests and runtime evidence;\n- external dependencies or authoritative external documentation; and\n- the rule to apply when sources disagree.\n\nState which facts were verified in the current task and which were inherited or require confirmation.\n\n### 5. Repository reality check\n\nBefore delegation or modification, capture:\n\n```text\nGit repository root:\nCanonical remote/repository:\nActive application:\nActive branch and HEAD:\nUpstream and ahead/behind/diverged state:\nComplete working-tree classification:\nCanonical startup command:\nRequired application port:\nRelevant focused and full validation commands:\nGenerated, archived, obsolete, or excluded trees:\nExisting uncommitted work and owner/provenance if known:\nSafe synchronization action taken or deferred:\n```\n\nFor this repository, verify rather than assume the current baseline. The expected canonical shape is one root Next.js application on `main`, normal startup through `npm run dev:full`, and normal development port `3000`. If reality differs, stop treating the expectation as fact and update the plan.\n\nDo not inspect secrets, overwrite local databases, or treat generated validation copies as source. Classify unrelated and pre-existing work before editing.\n\n### 6. Assumptions and unknowns\n\nUse this table:\n\n| ID  | Assumption or unknown | Impact if wrong | Verification method | Responsible lane | Can work proceed first? | Status/evidence |\n| --- | --------------------- | --------------- | ------------------- | ---------------- | ----------------------- | --------------- |\n| A1  |                       |                 |                     |                  | Yes/No                  |                 |\n\nDo not silently convert an unknown into a requirement or conclusion.\n\n### 7. Parallelization assessment\n\nAnswer explicitly:\n\n- Which work is meaningfully independent?\n- Which work depends on another result?\n- Which lanes can begin immediately?\n- Which steps must be serialized, and for what concrete reason?\n- Which resources are shared or exclusive?\n- Which files or interfaces are conflict-prone?\n- Which work should be read-only?\n- Which write lanes need isolated worktrees?\n- What is the maximum safe and useful concurrency under current capacity?\n- Why does the chosen decomposition improve speed, coverage, reliability, or context management without redundant work?\n- What conditions would cause a lane to be split, collapsed, cancelled, or serialized?\n\nFor a substantial task that remains primarily sequential, document the dependency, safety, exclusive-resource, or overlapping-file reason. Ease of coordination is not enough.\n\n### 8. Dependency graph\n\nRepresent lane identifiers, prerequisites, fan-out, fan-in, the critical path, and failure independence. A compact text or Mermaid graph is acceptable. For example:\n\n```text\nP0 repository preflight\n|-- R1 read-only subsystem inventory --\\\n|-- R2 read-only test/risk inventory ----+-- I1 coordinator reconciliation\n`-- R3 read-only requirements review ---/\n                                         |-- W1 isolated implementation lane --\\\n                                         `-- W2 isolated implementation lane --+-- I2 shared-file integration\n                                                                                 `-- V1 integrated validation\n                                                                                     `-- F1 finalization\n```\n\nAnnotate:\n\n- prerequisites and blocking relationships;\n- ready work and fan-out points;\n- integration/fan-in points;\n- the critical path;\n- serialized shared-resource operations; and\n- lanes that can continue if another lane fails.\n\nDo not force parallel work into a fake sequential checklist.\n\n### 9. Workstream matrix\n\n| Lane ID | Lane name | Objective | Agent role/capability | Read/write | Owned scope | Owned paths | Explicit non-scope | Dependencies | Shared resources | Deliverables | Required evidence/confidence | Focused validation | Status | Blockers |\n| ------- | --------- | --------- | --------------------- | ---------- | ----------- | ----------- | ------------------ | ------------ | ---------------- | ------------ | ---------------------------- | ------------------ | ------ | -------- |\n| R1      |           |           |                       | Read-only  |             |             |                    |              |                  |              |                              |                    | Ready  |          |\n\nEvery lane must have a non-overlapping evidence or edit boundary. If two write lanes need the same file, change the ownership or serialize the handoff before either edits.\n\n### 10. Subagent briefing template\n\nUse a bounded briefing such as:\n\n```text\nLane ID and role:\nObjective:\nBackground and governing requirements:\nScope:\nExplicit non-scope:\nAuthorization: read-only | write\nOwned concerns/paths:\nForbidden resources and paths:\nDependencies and prerequisite evidence:\nConstraints and invariants:\nWorker capability and reasoning requirement:\nRequired repository-wide searches or checks:\nRequired deliverable:\nRequired evidence (paths, symbols, commands, reproduction, confidence):\nFocused validation:\nReturn format (inspected, findings, paths/symbols, evidence, changes, tests, failures, uncertainty, overlaps/conflicts, recommendations, confidence, completion status):\nCompletion criteria:\nStop conditions:\nBlocker/uncertainty/overlap/ownership-conflict reporting:\n```\n\nBriefings must carry the requirements the worker needs; do not rely on the worker to reconstruct user intent. Require distilled results, not full raw logs. A worker must stop and report if its scope becomes ambiguous, overlaps another writer, requires a prohibited shared resource, or would broaden the task.\n\n### 11. Shared-resource and locking plan\n\n| Resource                             | Authoritative owner | Other permitted consumers            | Exclusive operations                        | Conflict-prevention method                              | Release/handoff condition                   |\n| ------------------------------------ | ------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |\n| Port `3000` and development server   |                     | Existing logs/HTTP evidence only     | Start/stop/restart                          | Inspect listener and recorded launcher state; one owner | Server stopped or ownership acknowledged    |\n| Browser session                      |                     | Read-only observers if safe          | Stateful automation                         | Separate contexts or one controller                     | State/evidence saved                        |\n| Database and seed state              |                     | Read-only queries if explicitly safe | Mutation/reset/seed/migration               | One database owner; back up when required               | State and evidence recorded                 |\n| Prisma schemas and migrations        |                     | Read-only review                     | Schema edit, migration creation/application | One authoritative writer; preserve order                | Schema/migration validated and handed off   |\n| `package.json` / `package-lock.json` |                     | Read-only review                     | Dependency install/regeneration             | One package owner                                       | Lockfile diff reviewed                      |\n| Global styles / layout / providers   |                     | Read-only review                     | Shared interface edits                      | One integration owner                                   | Consumers reconciled                        |\n| Route definitions                    |                     | Read-only inventory                  | Conflicting route/layout edits              | Path owner plus integration review                      | Route map validated                         |\n| Generated assets                     |                     | Consumers of existing output         | Regeneration                                | One generator, deterministic inputs                     | Output and provenance reviewed              |\n| Full E2E/build/validation            |                     | Consumers of reports                 | Expensive full run                          | One validation owner                                    | Results saved and reviewed                  |\n| Deployment environment               |                     | Read-only status consumers           | Deploy/rollback/config mutation             | Explicit authority and one operator                     | Health and rollback state confirmed         |\n| Chat/document synchronization        | Coordinator         | Workers provide summaries only       | Dry-run/live/validate/commit/push           | Run once after lanes finish                             | Structured report and remote state verified |\n\nAdd task-specific resources. In this repository, normal development uses `npm run dev:full` on `127.0.0.1:3000`. The Playwright default test port (`3100`) and validation production proof port (`3200`) are isolated validation resources, not extra canonical app instances.\n\n### 12. File ownership plan\n\nRecord:\n\n- one writer per file at a time;\n- path/component/package boundaries for every write lane;\n- the integration owner for shared files;\n- read-only reviewers assigned where edit overlap would otherwise occur;\n- worktree locations or isolation method for independent implementation lanes;\n- pre-existing files that no lane may stage, overwrite, or normalize;\n- ownership handoff order and evidence; and\n- the rule for newly discovered shared files.\n\nRecommended table:\n\n| Path or pattern | Writer/owner | Read-only reviewers | Ownership window | Dependencies | Handoff condition |\n| --------------- | ------------ | ------------------- | ---------------- | ------------ | ----------------- |\n|                 |              |                     |                  |              |                   |\n\nPackage manifests and lockfiles, Prisma schemas/migrations/seed, root layouts and providers, global styles/tokens, route entrypoints, central registries, runtime configuration, and generated files are presumed conflict-prone until inspected. Exactly one integration lane owns each such resource.\n\n### 13. Runtime strategy\n\nDefine:\n\n- who may start and stop the application;\n- the exact verified command;\n- how canonical port `3000` ownership is checked and maintained;\n- whether an already-running instance can be reused;\n- how other lanes request runtime evidence without starting duplicates;\n- how browser testing is divided and state isolated;\n- where server logs and screenshots are stored or referenced;\n- how stale or unknown processes are detected and handled safely; and\n- when runtime validation occurs relative to integration.\n\nRepository defaults:\n\n- use `npm run dev:full` and `npm run dev:stop` for the normal development lifecycle;\n- never kill an unknown listener merely to acquire port `3000`;\n- do not let workers run competing servers;\n- use Playwright's configured isolated server for E2E ownership, not the shared development server, unless the plan explicitly proves a safe alternative;\n- treat database-mutating browser work as exclusive unless separate databases are configured; and\n- centralize final browser/runtime review after integration.\n\n### 14. Testing strategy\n\nPlan all applicable layers:\n\n| Layer                 | Owner | Focused or integrated | Parallelizable shards    | Serialized resource      | Required evidence | Pass/fail treatment |\n| --------------------- | ----- | --------------------- | ------------------------ | ------------------------ | ----------------- | ------------------- |\n| Format/lint/type      |       |                       |                          |                          |                   |                     |\n| Unit/component        |       |                       |                          |                          |                   |                     |\n| Asset/contracts       |       |                       |                          |                          |                   |                     |\n| Integration/API       |       |                       |                          | Database/runtime if used |                   |                     |\n| E2E/accessibility     |       |                       |                          | Server/browser/database  |                   |                     |\n| Production build      |       | Integrated            | No duplicate full builds | Build cache/output       |                   |                     |\n| Performance           |       |                       |                          | Target runtime           |                   |                     |\n| Regression/acceptance |       | Integrated            |                          | Full gate                |                   |                     |\n\nUse only verified commands. Available focused commands include `npm run format:check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm test`, `npm run assets:validate`, `npm run test:e2e`, and `npm run build`. `npm run validate` is the full cross-cutting gate. No separate `test:integration` script is currently verified; describe the actual focused Vitest/Playwright coverage instead of inventing one.\n\nImplementation lanes run the smallest relevant checks. Independent unit shards may run concurrently. One validation owner deduplicates full lint/type/build/browser/server work and runs the required integrated gate on the combined state. Record commands, exit status, relevant counts, artifact paths, and failures. Classify failures as pre-existing, task regression, environmental, or unresolved with evidence; never erase failure evidence to make a gate appear clean.\n\n### 15. Integration strategy\n\nDefine:\n\n- integration owner;\n- lane handoff and merge order based on dependencies;\n- shared-file update owner and timing;\n- conflict-resolution process;\n- how duplicated or competing changes are detected;\n- how cross-lane assumptions and interfaces are checked;\n- how the complete integrated diff and working tree are reviewed; and\n- focused and cross-cutting checks to run after integration.\n\nThe integration owner preserves both sides of unexpected conflicts, consults the governing source, and resolves only when intent is clear. Workers do not casually merge, stage, or overwrite one another's work. Final Git reconciliation, if authorized, remains serialized.\n\n### 16. Risk register\n\n| Risk ID | Description | Probability  | Impact       | Detection method | Mitigation | Owner | Recovery action | Status |\n| ------- | ----------- | ------------ | ------------ | ---------------- | ---------- | ----- | --------------- | ------ |\n| K1      |             | Low/Med/High | Low/Med/High |                  |            |       |                 | Open   |\n\nInclude ownership collision, stale repository assumptions, accidental scope expansion, runtime/port/database contention, migration ordering, generated-file churn, pre-existing failures, security/privacy exposure, incomplete integration, and validation gaps where applicable.\n\n### 17. Decision log\n\n| Decision ID | Decision | Reason | Alternatives considered | Evidence | Affected lanes | Date/stage |\n| ----------- | -------- | ------ | ----------------------- | -------- | -------------- | ---------- |\n| D1          |          |        |                         |          |                |            |\n\nRecord concise rationale and evidence, not private chain-of-thought.\n\n### 18. Discovery log\n\n| Discovery ID | Finding | Evidence | Changes scope/dependencies/ownership/validation/risk | Plan update | Date/stage |\n| ------------ | ------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------- | ---------- |\n| X1           |         |          |                                                      |             |            |\n\nLog discoveries that materially change architecture assumptions, canonical locations, dependency edges, ownership, validation, risk, or completion criteria.\n\n### 19. Progress tracking\n\nUse these statuses consistently:\n\n- **Not ready** - prerequisites are unresolved.\n- **Ready** - prerequisites are satisfied and ownership is available.\n- **Active** - work is currently running.\n- **Blocked** - a specific blocker prevents progress.\n- **Awaiting integration** - lane deliverable is complete but not yet reconciled.\n- **Integrated** - combined into the coordinator's working state.\n- **Verified** - required focused and integrated checks passed or were accounted for.\n- **Complete** - acceptance criteria and reporting are finished.\n- **Cancelled as redundant** - lane is no longer necessary and useful partial evidence is preserved.\n\nPlans reflect actual state, not optimistic expectations. At most one owner writes a file or holds an exclusive resource at a time.\n\n### 20. Failure recovery\n\nFor each material lane or shared resource, define:\n\n- how partial results are preserved;\n- retry limit or retry condition;\n- when to narrow, split, or reassign a lane;\n- when an initially parallel lane must be serialized;\n- which dependent lanes become blocked;\n- which unaffected lanes continue;\n- how ownership and runtime locks are released;\n- recovery or rollback action for mutations; and\n- when user intervention is genuinely required.\n\nDo not restart completed work because another lane failed. A failed lane returns partial evidence and a precise blocker. The coordinator discloses any gap that remains material to completion.\n\n### 21. Final verification\n\nBefore claiming completion:\n\n- re-read the original user request and governing sources;\n- map every acceptance criterion to a delivered change or verified result;\n- reconcile every lane output and resolve or disclose contradictions;\n- confirm no material agent result remains unreviewed;\n- inspect the complete integrated diff and working tree for accidental scope expansion;\n- verify shared-file ownership and handoffs are complete;\n- run required focused, integrated, runtime, regression, accessibility, performance, security, migration, build, or acceptance checks as applicable;\n- distinguish skipped, unverified, pre-existing, and failed checks;\n- confirm generated artifacts and unrelated work were not accidentally staged or treated as source; and\n- run the single coordinator-owned repository finalization gate from `AGENTS.md`.\n\nFor documentation-only work, use proportionate document validation and do not launch the application or full test suite unless a governing instruction explicitly requires it.\n\n### 22. Final report format\n\nThe coordinator reports:\n\n```text\nOutcome: what was completed\nParallel execution: lanes used and why the decomposition was safe/useful\nAffected files/systems: task-authored changes, sync-owned changes, and pre-existing work classified separately\nValidation: commands/checks, exit status, and material evidence\nFailures/limitations: unresolved or skipped checks and why\nRemaining risks: known risks that remain within or outside scope\nFollow-up: only work genuinely outside the requested scope\nChat archive: required synchronization result\nDevelopment docs: required synchronization result\n```\n\nThe report provides concise rationale, evidence, decisions, and results. It does not expose private chain-of-thought or paste raw agent logs.\n\n## Standard parallel workflow patterns\n\nAdapt these patterns to the task rather than copying them mechanically.\n\n### Repository-wide audit\n\nTypical lanes include repository/architecture census, one or more subsystem reviews, runtime behavior, performance, accessibility, security, testing, data integrity, documentation consistency, missing opportunities, evidence verification, and final reconciliation. Target six to twelve workers when that many real categories exist. Preserve the audit prompt, taxonomy, severity definitions, evidence rules, and report format; give each lane a bounded evidence surface; verify severe/disputed findings; and centralize deduplication, severity reconciliation, and the final coherent audit.\n\n### Multi-feature implementation\n\n1. Establish shared architecture/contracts and identify shared files.\n2. Place genuinely independent feature lanes in separate worktrees or assign disjoint paths.\n3. Give shared architecture, schemas, configuration, manifests, and lockfiles one owner.\n4. Let each feature lane run focused tests.\n5. Integrate in dependency order under one owner.\n6. Run final full-system verification once on the combined state.\n\n### Bug investigation\n\nPotential lanes include independent reproduction, static code tracing, runtime tracing, regression history, test-gap analysis, platform-specific behavior, dependency behavior, and alternative-hypothesis verification. Keep hypotheses distinct and evidence-backed. The coordinator reconciles them and selects a supported root cause before authorizing implementation. Parallel fixes for the same suspected defect are normally redundant unless intentionally comparing isolated approaches.\n\n### Refactor\n\nStart architecture mapping, consumer inventory, and test-baseline lanes in parallel. Serialize shared-interface changes under one owner, then fan out independent package/component migrations with disjoint ownership. Integrate in dependency order and run compatibility/regression verification after all consumers move.\n\n### Frontend experience work\n\nPotential lanes include route/shell structure, navigation, bounded component groups, animation/interaction, design-system consistency, responsive behavior, accessibility, performance, browser reproduction, test coverage, visual verification, and integration review. Use up to twelve when these are genuinely independent. Assign one owner to root layout, providers, shared tokens/global styles, and route shells, plus one runtime/browser owner. Finish with an integrated responsive, keyboard, motion-mode, console, performance, and UX review against the original acceptance criteria.\n\n### Backend or database work\n\nPotential lanes include API, services, repositories, schema analysis, data integrity, security, tests, operational readiness, observability, and documentation. Separate them only where evidence or files do not overlap. Give schemas and the ordered migration chain one authoritative writer and one database/migration operator. Serialize migration generation/application and shared state mutation. Integrate API/service contracts before final database, security, regression, and deployment-readiness validation.\n\n### Documentation task\n\nPotential lanes include source inventory, contradiction review, terminology, factual verification, structural review, instruction-discovery validation, configuration validation, and editorial integration. Fan out read-only discovery and fact-checking by topic. Topic-specific drafting may run in parallel only with disjoint document ownership. Use one editorial integration owner for shared documents, terminology, links, and final formatting. Verify claims against sources and run proportionate diff/format/link checks. Do not create extra helper documents when the requested output is fixed.\n\n### Homogeneous batch investigations\n\nWhen the installed client exposes `spawn_agents_on_csv` or a verified equivalent, optional batch fan-out may handle many independent items with the same analysis: one component, route, package, animation system, test failure, migration target, document, or incident per row. Treat this capability as client-surface dependent and experimental; use ordinary named lanes when clearer.\n\nEach batch requires a stable item ID, explicit input columns, fixed output schema, scope and non-scope, evidence fields, confidence, exactly one result per worker, bounded runtime, maximum safe concurrency, consolidated output, coordinator reconciliation, and retained error rows. Never silently drop failures or assume a batch tool exists without verifying the current client.\n\n## Anti-patterns\n\nDo not:\n\n- process independent areas sequentially without a concrete reason;\n- spawn agents with substantially identical scopes;\n- make every agent scan the entire repository;\n- launch work before evidence, path, and resource ownership are defined;\n- allow multiple agents to edit the same file concurrently;\n- let multiple development servers compete for port `3000`;\n- make every lane run the entire test suite;\n- use agent count as a quality metric or create microscopic artificial lanes;\n- ignore slower-lane results because faster lanes finished first;\n- combine reports without reconciling duplicates, severity, or contradictions;\n- let subagents reinterpret or broaden user requirements;\n- use parallelism to bypass safety, review, authorization, or validation;\n- restart completed work after one lane fails;\n- claim completion before integration and verification;\n- create alternate application copies, port-specific versions, or abandoned variants;\n- use generated snapshots or obsolete/source-history trees because they are easier to discover;\n- hide uncertainty, partial failure, skipped validation, or incomplete integration;\n- let workers independently fetch/rebase/merge/commit/push or run chat/document synchronization without explicit exclusive ownership; or\n- stage the whole repository by default in a dirty worktree.\n\n## Adaptive plan behavior\n\nUpdate the plan when:\n\n- new dependencies or shared resources are discovered;\n- two lanes overlap or shared-file conflict becomes likely;\n- a lane becomes unnecessary or duplicates another;\n- supposedly independent work is found to be dependent;\n- a blocked lane can be narrowed or split;\n- additional safe parallelism becomes available;\n- runtime evidence contradicts static analysis;\n- the canonical implementation differs from the initial assumption;\n- validation reveals a pre-existing failure or regression; or\n- risk, completion criteria, or user authority changes.\n\nWhen adapting:\n\n1. preserve completed work and useful partial evidence;\n2. update dependency edges, ownership, locks, status, risk, and validation;\n3. notify affected lanes and stop conflicting writers before reassignment;\n4. record the material discovery or decision;\n5. start newly ready independent work promptly; and\n6. avoid unnecessary restarts.\n\nThe plan serves the task. It may evolve, but it may never override the user's instructions or governing product requirements.\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

The credential gate still blocks edits, tests, and API-backed implementation until resolved; it does not block read-only retrieval of current model, migration, or prompting guidance.\r\n\r\n## First action for latest-model changes\r\n\r\nBefore reading memory, inspecting the repo, fetching docs, or checking API credentials, classify the request:\r\n\r\n- **Latest/current prompting guidance, or change requested + latest/current/newest/recommended/default/flagship/unspecified target:** immediately run the resolver with the platform-specific command below and inspect its JSON output. This includes asking how to prompt the latest model, changing prompts, a model picker, model references, an SDK integration, replacing an older named model with \"the current model\", or asking \"which model should I migrate/upgrade to?\". Do not directly fetch `latest-model.md` for this branch.\r\n- **Pure model-selection question only, with no prompting guidance or requested change:** directly fetch `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model.md`; do not run the resolver.\r\n- **Change requested + explicit target model:** preserve that target; do not run the latest-model resolver. Do not substitute latest-model guidance.\r\n\r\nRun the resolver without relying on filesystem executable bits:\r\n\r\n- POSIX shells: `sh <skill-dir>/scripts/resolve-latest-model-info`\r\n- Windows: run the CommonJS implementation with Node.js 18 or newer: `node <skill-dir>\\scripts\\resolve-latest-model-info.cjs`. If a fetched guide contains only a title or no substantive body, retry the exact markdown URL through MCP/search fallback; if that also fails, use bundled fallback references and disclose the fallback.\r\n\r\n## OpenAI product snapshots\r\n\r\n1. Apps SDK: Build ChatGPT apps by providing a web component UI and an MCP server that exposes your app's tools to ChatGPT.\r\n2. Responses API: A unified endpoint designed for stateful, multimodal, tool-using interactions in agentic workflows.\r\n3. Chat Completions API: Generate a model response from a list of messages comprising a conversation.\r\n4. Codex: OpenAI's coding agent for software development that can write, understand, review, and debug code.\r\n5. gpt-oss: Open-weight OpenAI reasoning models (gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b) released under the Apache 2.0 license.\r\n6. Realtime API: Build low-latency, multimodal experiences including natural speech-to-speech conversations.\r\n7. Agents SDK: A toolkit for building agentic apps where a model can use tools and context, hand off to other agents, stream partial results, and keep a full trace.\r\n\r\n## Codex self-knowledge\r\n\r\nUse this path for questions about Codex itself: configuring, extending, operating, troubleshooting, local state, product surfaces, or where Codex behavior should live. A codebase merely mentioning a plugin, skill, hook, MCP server, browser, or automation is not enough. For generic software tasks, answer the software task directly; if asked whether Codex self-knowledge applies, answer that meta question briefly and continue the requested artifact.\r\n\r\n### Source Route\r\n\r\nThe Codex manual is the first source for broad Codex synthesis. Treat the manual and Docs MCP as different lanes, not interchangeable official-doc sources. For published-user Codex product answers, the source route is complete: the manual, Docs MCP when this route calls for it, official OpenAI web fallback, and callable capabilities surfaced in the current session when the question is about that capability. Knowledge bases outside developers.openai.com are outside this route for public product answers.\r\n\r\nFor broad Codex behavior, setup, customization, skills, plugins, MCP, hooks, `AGENTS.md`, automations, surfaces, local state, or system-map questions:\r\n\r\n1. Reuse a same-thread manual and outline path when it is still fresh.\r\n2. Otherwise run the skill-local helper first in normal writable sessions. Skip it without trying only when the session is explicitly read-only, shell execution is unavailable, or visible policy shows no allowed temp cache.\r\n3. By default, the helper chooses the first usable temp cache dir in this order: `$TMPDIR/openai-docs-cache`, `%TEMP%\\openai-docs-cache`, `%TMP%\\openai-docs-cache`, `/private/tmp/openai-docs-cache`, then `/tmp/openai-docs-cache`. Workspace-only write access is not enough for this temp cache.\r\n4. Run the helper directly unless you need to override the cache dir. The helper falls back to `curl` when native `fetch` is unavailable or when proxy env vars are present, so no shell-specific proxy prefix is required. Resolve `<skill-dir>` to this skill's actual directory; in copied local eval workdirs this is usually `.codex/skills/openai-docs`:\r\n\r\n```bash\r\nnode <skill-dir>/scripts/fetch-codex-manual.mjs\r\n```\r\n\r\nIf you need to override the cache dir, pass `--cache-dir <cache-dir>`. On Windows, the helper checks `%TEMP%` and `%TMP%` automatically; in PowerShell, `$env:TEMP\\\\openai-docs-cache` is a typical explicit override.\r\n\r\nTreat helper availability as established by explicit read-only/no-shell policy or an actual command result. A guessed sandbox or guessed helper failure is not enough to switch to Docs MCP or web lookup; after an actual helper command failure, continue to the narrowest official next source below.\r\n\r\nThe helper verifies freshness, writes `codex-manual.md`, and emits `codex-manual.outline.md`. The outline maps source pages and headings to line ranges; use it to choose the relevant manual section, then read or search targeted manual sections for Codex product facts. Use the skill directory to locate and run the helper; after the helper succeeds, use the returned manual and outline paths as the search scope for Codex product facts and term coverage checks.\r\n\r\nReuse the same-thread manual and outline paths for follow-up Codex questions. Refresh first when the manual was fetched more than about a day ago, the path is unusable, the path came from another thread or uncertain provenance, or likely-current information is missing and staleness is plausible.\r\n\r\nFor questions about whether the manual is current enough to rely on now, run the helper when temp caching is allowed and base the answer on its returned status, manual path, and outline path.\r\n\r\nIf the manual resolves a Codex claim, answer from it and stop expanding sources for that claim; continue the user's broader task if the docs lookup was only one dependency. Manual source pages and known anchors are enough citation support for manual-covered material.\r\n\r\nIf the helper is skipped because the session is read-only, has no shell execution, or has no allowed temp cache, the next source is Docs MCP: call `mcp__openaiDeveloperDocs__search_openai_docs`, then `mcp__openaiDeveloperDocs__fetch_openai_doc` for a relevant hit before any web fallback.\r\n\r\nIf a user names a Codex term or mode that a fresh manual does not use, search the manual for obvious adjacent concepts, then answer that the exact term is not documented and use the closest documented terminology. If the prompt asks how that term maps to Codex behavior, resolve the mapping from adjacent manual sections. If the exact term remains material or likely current after that manual pass, use one narrow Docs MCP search/fetch before bounded uncertainty; otherwise, the source lookup for that terminology or mapping claim is complete.\r\n\r\nUse the narrowest official next source only when the manual is unavailable, the helper fails, temp caching is not allowed, another material claim is missing or likely stale, or the user explicitly needs a page-specific citation. Prefer one specific Docs MCP search and, if it returns a clearly relevant page, one fetch; for unresolved Codex capability names, acronyms, scheduling terms, or exact error text, this Docs MCP step is the next source before web search. After the manual plus any permitted Docs MCP gap-fill, resolve remaining gaps as bounded uncertainty. Use official-domain web fallback only after that Docs MCP path is unavailable or unhelpful. If the claim is still not established, stop with bounded uncertainty. If official docs/manual conflict with a callable capability already surfaced in the current session, state the conflict and prefer verified current-session behavior for that environment.\r\n\r\nFor undocumented or private-looking model slugs, product mode labels, entitlement labels, account access paths, or rollout names, answer from current public docs and bounded uncertainty. Those labels are not a reason to leave the public source route.\r\n\r\nFor support-style diagnostics, prefer a layer-by-layer answer from the manual over provider-specific web lookups: installed/enabled plugin, bundled app or connector authorization, MCP setup, workspace/admin policy, restart or new-thread expectations, then support or feedback if still unresolved.\r\n\r\nIf the source route still does not establish a claim, return bounded uncertainty or route to support, an admin, or product feedback instead of widening the investigation.\r\n\r\nFor unresolved product terminology, answer from the manual plus the allowed official next source. If those sources do not establish the term, answer with bounded uncertainty from those sources.\r\n\r\n### Surface Map\r\n\r\nWhen Codex nouns or durable-instruction surfaces overlap, recommend the smallest surface that matches the scope:\r\n\r\n- Prompt or thread context -> one-off task constraints.\r\n- `AGENTS.md` -> durable repo conventions, commands, verification steps, and review expectations; closer nested files apply under their subtree.\r\n- Project `.codex/config.toml` -> trusted-repo Codex settings such as sandbox, MCP, hooks, model, or reasoning defaults.\r\n- Global config or global guidance -> personal defaults across repos.\r\n- Skill -> reusable task workflow with references or scripts.\r\n- Plugin -> installable bundle with skills plus commands, tools, MCP config, hooks, assets, apps, or marketplace metadata.\r\n- MCP server or app connector -> live external data/actions or authorized private app/workspace data. Use connectors for private Google Docs, Calendar, Slack, GitHub, Notion, and similar data instead of web search or model memory.\r\n- Automation -> scheduled checks, reminders, monitors, or follow-up work; use a thread heartbeat when continuity in an existing thread matters.\r\n- Hook -> lifecycle enforcement around tool calls, commands, or file edits.\r\n\r\nSplit mixed-scope requests instead of forcing one answer. Example: \"always do X, but only for this PR\" defaults to prompt/thread context for the current run; use `AGENTS.md` or project config only if it should persist, hooks only for mechanical enforcement, and automations only for scheduled or follow-up work.\r\n\r\nUse this quick product map when needed: CLI is terminal-first local repo work; IDE extension is editor-attached coding; Codex app is desktop planning, review, and interactive work; cloud/web is hosted parallel/offloaded work; Browser Use/in-app browser is Codex-controlled web testing; Chrome extension uses the user's Chrome profile; Computer Use controls desktop apps and OS UI. Keep `config.toml` defaults, `requirements.toml` constraints, and managed/admin policy separate.\r\n\r\n### Boundaries And Output\r\n\r\n- API key auth does not imply ChatGPT, cloud task, or connector access. For plugin/app/auth failures, check bundle availability, plugin installed/enabled state, connector/app authorization, MCP setup, restart/refresh expectations, workspace policy, and per-surface availability before answering.\r\n- Sandbox or network denials need scoped escalation with a clear justification. Destructive commands, writes outside the workspace, or broad access changes require explicit approval.\r\n- Memory can provide user preference or context, but explicit prompt instructions win and memory is not a source for current external facts.\r\n- For affirmative surface-selection answers, use this shape: recommendation, why, what to avoid, and the manual/source evidence used.\r\n- When page-specific Codex citations are actually needed, these anchors often fit: `concepts/customization#agents-guidance` for `AGENTS.md`, `concepts/customization#skills` for skills, `plugins/build#plugin-structure` for plugins, `concepts/customization#mcp` for MCP, `config-advanced#hooks` for hooks, `app/automations#thread-automations` for thread automations, and `config-reference#configtoml` for config.\r\n\r\n## If MCP server is missing\r\n\r\nIf MCP tools fail or no OpenAI docs resources are available:\r\n\r\n1. Run the install command yourself: `codex mcp add openaiDeveloperDocs --url https://developers.openai.com/mcp`\r\n2. If it fails due to permissions/sandboxing, immediately retry the same command with escalated permissions and include a 1-sentence justification for approval.\r\n3. Ask the user to run the install command only if the escalated attempt fails.\r\n4. Ask the user to restart Codex.\r\n5. Re-run the doc search/fetch after restart.\r\n\r\n## Workflow\r\n\r\n1. Clarify whether the request is general docs lookup, model selection, a model-string upgrade, prompt-upgrade guidance, or broader API/provider migration.\r\n2. For Codex self-knowledge requests, follow the Codex self-knowledge source procedure above.\r\n3. For model-selection or upgrade requests, prefer current remote docs over bundled references when the user asks for latest/current/default guidance.\r\n   - For docs-only model-selection questions, fetch `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model.md`, find the latest model ID and explicit migration or prompt-guidance links, and prefer explicit links over derived URLs.\r\n   - For explicit named-model requests, preserve the requested model target. Mention newer remote guidance only as optional.\r\n   - For latest/current/default prompting guidance or dynamic upgrades, run the resolver with the platform-specific command above as the first docs operation before any direct latest-model.md fetch or API-key credential gate, then fetch both returned guide URLs directly when possible.\r\n   - Fetch returned guide URLs exactly; do not derive or substitute model-specific URLs.\r\n   - If a direct guide fetch fails or returns only a title, use the developer-docs MCP tools or official OpenAI-domain search to find the same guide content.\r\n   - If remote docs are unavailable, use bundled fallback references and say that fallback guidance was used.\r\n4. For model upgrades, keep changes behavior-preserving and scoped: update active OpenAI API model defaults, directly related prompts, and the registries, routing, pricing, capability, or picker surfaces that the user actually placed in scope.\r\n5. Leave historical docs, examples, eval baselines, fixtures, provider comparisons, intentionally pinned fallbacks, and ambiguous older model usage unchanged unless the user explicitly asks to upgrade them. Do not collapse a multi-model router or picker into one flagship model; preserve the existing cost, latency, and quality roles.\r\n6. Keep SDK, tooling, IDE, plugin, shell, auth, and provider-environment migrations out of a model-and-prompt upgrade unless the user explicitly asks for them.\r\n7. If a safe upgrade needs API-surface changes, schema rewiring, tool-handler changes, or broader implementation work, classify it explicitly. Make those changes when implementation is within the user's requested scope; otherwise report the exact blocker or confirmation needed instead of silently changing behavior.\r\n8. For general docs lookup, start with a compact, title-like search query of 2-6 essential terms. Do not turn the full user question into a keyword list. Fetch the best page and exact section needed, and answer with concise citations.\r\n\r\n## Reference map\r\n\r\nRead only what you need:\r\n\r\n- `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model.md` -> current model-selection and \"best/latest/current model\" questions.\r\n- `scripts/fetch-codex-manual.mjs` -> current Codex manual fetch, verification, local temp cache, and outline generation.\r\n- `https://developers.openai.com/codex/codex-manual.md` -> current Codex self-knowledge synthesis, including setup, customization, skills, plugins, MCP, hooks, `AGENTS.md`, automations, and surface behavior; normally access it through the helper path and targeted file reads when temp caching is available.\r\n- `references/latest-model.md` -> bundled fallback for model-selection and \"best/latest/current model\" questions.\r\n- `references/upgrade-guide.md` -> bundled routing fallback for model upgrade and upgrade-planning requests.\r\n- `references/upgrading-to-gpt-5p6-sol.md` -> GPT-5.6 Sol/family migration judgment, compatibility gates, optional feature boundaries, and validation.\r\n- `references/prompting-guide.md` -> bundled GPT-5.6 prompting fallback plus the live Prompting Best Practices extraction contract.\r\n\r\n## Quality rules\r\n\r\n- Treat OpenAI docs as the source of truth; avoid speculation.\r\n- For Codex self-knowledge, follow the source route above instead of relying on remembered behavior.\r\n- Keep migration changes narrow and behavior-preserving.\r\n- Prefer prompt-only upgrades when possible.\r\n- Avoid inventing pricing, availability, parameters, API changes, or breaking changes.\r\n- Keep quotes short and within policy limits; prefer paraphrase with citations.\r\n- If multiple pages differ, call out the difference and cite both.\r\n- If official docs and verified callable current-session behavior disagree, state the conflict before making broad claims or edits.\r\n- If docs do not cover the userâ€™s need, say so and offer next steps.\r\n\r\n## Tooling notes\r\n\r\n- Use MCP doc tools before web search for OpenAI-related markdown docs. The Codex manual flow is the exception: follow the Codex self-knowledge source procedure for broad Codex synthesis.\r\n- If the MCP server is installed but returns no meaningful results, then use web search as a fallback.\r\n- When falling back to web search, restrict to official OpenAI domains (developers.openai.com, platform.openai.com) and cite sources.\r\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

const matches = ALL_TOOLS.filter(x => /openaiDeveloperDocs|openai docs|Codex manual/i.test(`${x.name} ${x.description}`));
text(matches);

Page\nL747: # Configuration Reference\nL748: \nL749: Complete reference for Codex config.toml and requirements.toml\nL750: \nL751: Copy Page\nL752: \nL753: Use this page as a searchable reference for Codex configuration files. For conceptual guidance and examples, start with cite259†Config basics and cite260†Advanced Config .\nL754: ## `config.toml`\nL755: \nL756: User-level configuration lives in `~/.codex/config.toml`. You can also add project-scoped overrides in `.codex/config.toml` files. Codex loads project-scoped config files only when you trust the project.\nL757: Project-scoped config can’t override machine-local provider, auth, host-owned app request metadata, notification, configuration profile selection, or telemetry routing keys. Codex ignores `openai_base_url`, `chatgpt_base_url`, `apps_mcp_product_sku`, `model_provider`, `model_providers`, `notify`, `profile`, `profiles`, `experimental_realtime_ws_base_url`, and `otel` when they appear in a project-local `.codex/config.toml`; put provider, notification, and telemetry keys in user-level config instead.\nL758: Config cite388†profile files live next to `config.toml` as `$CODEX_HOME/profile-name.config.toml`; select one with `--profile profile-name`.\nL759: For sandbox and approval keys (`approval_policy`, `sandbox_mode`, and `sandbox_workspace_write.*`), pair this reference with cite389†Sandbox and approvals , cite390†Protected paths in writable roots , and cite391†Network access . For beta permission profiles, see cite295†Permissions .\nL760: \nL761: Key Type / Values Details\nL762: `agents.<name>.config_file ``string (path)`\nL763: \nL764: Path to a TOML config layer for that role; relative paths resolve from the config file that declares the role.\nL765: \nL766: `agents.<name>.description ``string `\nL767: Role guidance shown to Codex when choosing and spawning that agent type.\nL768: \nL769: `agents.<name>.nickname_candidates ``array<string>`\nL770: \nL771: Optional pool of display nicknames for spawned agents in that role.\nL772: \nL773: `agents.interrupt_message ``boolean `\nL774: \nL775: Record a model-visible message when an agent turn is interrupted (default: true).\nL776: \nL777: `agents.job_max_runtime_seconds ``number `\nL778: \nL779: Default per-worker timeout for `spawn_agents_on_csv` jobs. When unset, the tool falls back to 1800 seconds per worker.\nL780: `agents.max_depth ``number `\nL781: \nL782: Maximum nesting depth allowed for spawned agent threads (root sessions start at depth 0; default: 1).\nL783: \nL784: `agents.max_threads ``number `\nL785: \nL786: Maximum number of agent threads that can be open concurrently. Defaults to `6` when unset.\nL787: \n", "type": "input_text"}]

Windows\nL741: \nL742:   * cite270† Desktop app  L743:   * cite271† Windows sandbox  L744:   * cite272† WSL  L745: \nL746: cite390†Image ChatGPT desktop app\nL747: \nL748: Copy Page\nL749: # Subagents\nL750: \nL751: Use subagents in ChatGPT and Codex, and configure custom Codex agents\nL752: \nL753: cite391†Image ChatGPT desktop app\nL754: \nL755: Copy Page\nL756: \nL757: Work mode and Codex can run subagent workflows by spawning specialized agents in parallel and then collecting their results in one response. This can be particularly helpful for complex tasks that are highly parallel, such as codebase exploration or implementing a multi-step feature plan.\nL758: In local Codex clients, you can also define custom agents with different model configurations and instructions for different tasks.\nL759: ## Availability\nL760: \nL761: Work mode exposes subagent workflows and activity to eligible accounts.\nL762: \nL763: Current Codex releases enable subagent workflows by default. Subagent activity appears in the ChatGPT desktop app, Codex CLI, and the IDE extension.\nL764: \nL765: Because each subagent does its own model and tool work, subagent workflows consume more tokens than comparable single-agent runs.\nL766: In Work mode, ask ChatGPT to delegate independent work to subagents. The agents run in ChatGPT’s hosted environment, and the chat shows their activity and results. At most intelligence levels, ask for delegation explicitly. With Ultra, ChatGPT can proactively delegate work when parallel agents would materially improve speed or quality.\nL767: Ask Codex in an app chat to delegate independent parts of the work to subagents. Current local Codex releases delegate when you ask directly or when applicable `AGENTS.md` or skill instructions request it. The app surfaces each subagent thread so you can inspect its work and the summary returned to the main chat.\nL768: Ask Codex in an interactive CLI session to use subagents. Codex can also follow applicable `AGENTS.md` or skill instructions that request delegation. Use `/agent` to inspect and switch between agent threads while they run. The main thread collects the subagent results into its final response.\nL769: Ask Codex in an IDE chat to delegate independent parts of the work to subagents. Codex can also follow applicable `AGENTS.md` or skill instructions that request delegation. When the background-agent UI is available, active subagents appear above the composer. Expand the panel to see their status, stop all active subagents, or open an individual subagent thread.\nL770: ## Why subagent workflows help\nL771: \nL772: Even with large context windows, models have limits. If you flood the main chat (where you’re defining requirements, constraints, and decisions) with noisy intermediate output such as exploration notes, test logs, stack traces, and command output, the session can become less reliable over time.\nL773: \nL774: This is often described as:\nL775:   * Context pollution: useful information gets buried under noisy intermediate output.\nL776:   * Context rot: performance degrades as the chat fills up with less relevant details.\nL777: \n", "type": "input_text"}]

  {ref_id:"turn2view0","pattern":"max_threads"},
  {ref_id:"turn2view0","pattern":"max_depth"},
  {ref_id:"turn2view0","pattern":"concurrent"}
],response_length:"medium"}); text(r);
