Subagent communication plan\r\n+\r\n+Subagent use must never be invisible to the user. Every Tier 1 or Tier 2 plan that uses workers must include a communication plan covering the items below. If a Tier 0 task uses a distinct verification or other worker, the same launch and lifecycle disclosures apply even though the full planning template does not. These rules apply to one worker, multiple specialists, full-capacity fan-out, later waves, replacements, verification lanes, read-only or write lanes, test lanes, and batch workers.\r\n+\r\n+#### Initial manifest format and launch timing\r\n+\r\n+Before or immediately after launching workers, provide a visible chat update titled `Subagent started`, `Subagents started`, or an equally clear wave-specific title. Never omit the disclosure for a single worker. Use a compact structured card for one worker and one grouped table for workers launched at approximately the same time.\r\n+\r\n+For each worker, the launch update must disclose:\r\n+\r\n+- lane ID and visible subagent name or role;\r\n+- current status, primary objective, end goal, and why the lane exists;\r\n+- read-only or write-enabled mode;\r\n+- owned subsystem, concern, files, paths, components, or evidence boundary;\r\n+- explicit non-scope, forbidden paths, and prohibited resources;\r\n+- expected deliverable and completion criteria;\r\n+- dependencies and prerequisite evidence;\r\n+- shared resources it may not control and their authoritative owners;\r\n+- how the coordinator will review and integrate the output; and\r\n+- expected capacity, quality, coverage, verification, specialization, or elapsed-time benefit.\r\n+\r\n+For each write-enabled worker, the visible launch update must make ownership unmistakable: list the exact files, path patterns, components, or packages it may edit; exact shared files it may not edit; whether it has an isolated worktree; the integration owner; and the handoff condition. If those boundaries cannot yet be stated clearly, announce and keep the lane read-only until they can.\r\n+\r\n+When several workers launch together, use a manifest such as:\r\n+\r\n+```text\r\n+Subagents started: <count>\r\n+\r\n+| Lane | Role | Status | Mode | Ownership | End goal/deliverable | Non-scope | Dependencies | Integration | Expected benefit |\r\n+|------|------|--------|------|-----------|----------------------|-----------|--------------|-------------|------------------|\r\n+```\r\n+\r\n+For one worker, use a compact card containing the same information rather than omitting fields because a table seems unnecessary.\r\n+\r\n+Every launch update must also state:\r\n+\r\n+- total workers launched in that update and direct-worker slots still available;\r\n+- work retained by the coordinator;\r\n+- which applicable shared resources remain centralized and their authoritative owners;\r\n+- lanes that are blocked or queued; and\r\n+- whether additional workers will start when capacity opens.\r\n+\r\n+The coordinator explicitly retains accountability for the original user requirements, decomposition, dependency tracking, conflict prevention, major decisions, contradiction resolution, integration, final validation, final response, Git integration, and repository synchronization. Launching workers never transfers accountability for the final result.\r\n+\r\n+#### Grouping and meaningful status-change triggers\r\n+\r\n+Group workers launched or completed at approximately the same time into one structured update. Do not send one nearly identical message per worker. Send a new grouped or individual update only when something meaningful changes, including:\r\n+\r\n+- a worker launches or a new wave begins;\r\n+- ownership, objective, permissions, dependencies, or end goal materially changes;\r\n+- a worker becomes blocked, fails, or reports a major finding;\r\n+- a worker completes, is replaced, is cancelled as redundant, or releases its slot;\r\n+- ownership transfers; or\r\n+- integration begins or materially changes state.\r\n+\r\n+Later workers require their own clearly labeled manifest, such as `Subagent wave 2 started`, `Additional verification lanes started`, or `Replacement lane started`; an earlier manifest never covers newly created workers.\r\n+\r\n+#### Completion-report format\r\n+\r\n+When one or more workers finish at approximately the same time, report their results in a compact card or table. For every completed lane, state:\r\n+\r\n+- lane ID and role;\r\n+- completion status and deliverable received;\r\n+- major finding or authorized change;\r\n+- focused validation performed;\r\n+- unresolved uncertainty;\r\n+- whether the coordinator has reviewed the output; whether it is awaiting integration, integrated, or independently verified; and\r\n+- confirmation that the worker slot was released.\r\n+\r\n+Worker-reported completion is not coordinator verification. Preserve the distinction among worker complete, output reviewed, output integrated, and output independently verified in both the chat update and workstream matrix.\r\n+\r\n+#### Blocker, failure, replacement, and cancellation format\r\n+\r\n+For a blocked or failed worker, disclose the lane ID, exact blocker or failure, useful partial work preserved, ownership or resource locks released, impact on dependent lanes, confirmation that unrelated lanes continue when applicable, recovery action, and whether the lane will be retried, narrowed, split, reassigned, serialized, replaced, or cancelled.\r\n+\r\n+Do not hide a failed or blocked lane because other lanes succeeded.\r\n+\r\n+For a replacement lane, announce the replacement with a new or clearly related lane identity, its complete launch manifest, what prompted the replacement, preserved partial evidence, and the remaining responsibility transferred to it.\r\n+\r\n+For a lane cancelled as redundant or no longer necessary, disclose why it became redundant, useful output preserved, the lane that now owns remaining responsibility, the released slot or resource, and confirmation that no unreviewed material result was discarded.\r\n+\r\n+#### Scope and ownership-change format\r\n+\r\n+Before a worker continues under revised ownership, objective, permissions, dependencies, or end goal, announce:\r\n+\r\n+- the previous scope and new scope;\r\n+- the reason for the change;\r\n+- whether ownership conflicts were checked;\r\n+- the other lanes or authoritative resource owners affected; and\r\n+- whether the dependency graph, workstream matrix, file/resource ownership plan, or another plan section was updated.\r\n+\r\n+Workers may not silently broaden their scope. A write-ownership transfer stops the prior writer, records the handoff condition and evidence, updates the one-writer record, and announces the transfer before the new writer edits.\r\n+\r\n+#### Anti-spam and reasoning boundary\r\n+\r\n+Do not require or send updates for every tool call, file opened, search, minor internal observation, unchanged poll, or the mere passage of time. Do not repeat `still working` messages without a meaningful topology, ownership, status, finding, dependency, or integration change. Do not expose or request private chain-of-thought, raw internal reasoning, unfiltered worker logs, or full worker prompts unless the user explicitly requests the prompt text. Visible updates provide decisions, scope, evidence, results, and concise rationale sufficient for the user to understand and audit the parallel work.\r\n+\r\n+#### Final parallel-execution summary\r\n+\r\n+For every substantial task that used subagents, the final response must concisely summarize:\r\n+\r\n+- total unique subagents used, maximum simultaneous workers, and worker waves;\r\n+- roles and ownership boundaries, including which lanes were read-only and which modified files;\r\n+- important results from each lane;\r\n+- failed, blocked, replaced, or cancelled lanes and the disposition of partial work;\r\n+- how outputs, contradictions, overlaps, and handoffs were reconciled;\r\n+- whether parallelization increased useful capacity or throughput, technical depth, coverage, verification confidence, or reduced elapsed time; and\r\n+- avoidable duplication or coordination overhead observed and improvements for the next decomposition.\r\n+\r\n+Keep the summary evidence-based and readable. When several workers launch at approximately the same time, announce them together in one compact table rather than sending one repetitive message per worker. A launch manifest must disclose, for every lane:\n+\n+- **Lane ID**;\n+- **subagent name or role**;\n+- **status**;\n+- **primary objective** and user-visible end goal;\n+- **why the lane exists**;\n+- **read-only or write-enabled mode**;\n+- **owned subsystem, concern, exact files, path patterns, components, packages, or evidence boundary**;\n+- **explicit non-scope and prohibited files, paths, systems, and resources**;\n+- **expected deliverable**;\n+- **completion criteria**;\n+- **dependencies and prerequisite evidence**;\n+- **shared resources it may not control**;\n+- **how the coordinator will review and integrate its output**; and\n+- **expected capacity, quality, coverage, verification, or elapsed-time benefit**.\n+\n+For a multi-worker launch, use a compact structure equivalent to:\n+\n+| Lane | Role            | Status | Mode      | Ownership                     | End goal and deliverable                | Non-scope                                | Dependencies         | Integration                                           | Expected benefit                     |\n+| ---- | --------------- | ------ | --------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |\n+| R1   | Route inventory | Active | Read-only | `src/app` route-tree evidence | Verified route and transition inventory | No edits, server, database, or Git state | Repository preflight | Coordinator cross-checks against other route evidence | Faster, more complete route coverage |\n+\n+The launch update must also state:\n+\n+- the total number of workers launched and the number of direct-worker slots still available;\n+- what work remains with the coordinator;\n+- which shared or expensive resources have one authoritative owner;\n+- which lanes are blocked or queued; and\n+- whether additional workers will start when capacity opens or dependencies clear.\n+\n+When only one worker launches, disclosure remains mandatory. Use a compact card or structured block that provides the same information, for example:\n+\n+> **Subagent started — V1: Runtime verification**\n+>\n+> - **Status:** Active\n+> - **Mode:** Read-only\n+> - **Owns:** Browser reproduction and runtime evidence\n+> - **Objective and end goal:** Confirm the reported transition defect and capture evidence\n+> - **Why this lane exists:** Independent runtime verification while static analysis continues\n+> - **Must not modify or control:** Application code, database, port ownership, or Git state\n+> - **Deliverable and completion:** Reproduction steps, affected routes, console evidence, and confidence after the defined checks complete\n+> - **Dependencies:** Existing runnable application state\n+> - **Integration:** Coordinator compares the runtime evidence with static findings\n+> - **Expected benefit:** Higher confidence without blocking implementation analysis\n+\n+### Ownership and shared-resource transparency\n+\n+The visible manifest must make write ownership unmistakable. For every write-enabled lane, identify the exact files, path patterns, components, or packages it may edit; the exact shared files it may not edit; whether it has an isolated worktree; its integration owner; and its handoff condition. If ownership cannot be stated clearly, the worker remains read-only until the boundary is resolved and visibly announced. Exactly one agent may write a file at a time, and the existing exclusive-resource rules remain in force.\n+\n+Whenever applicable, the launch or ownership update must identify the authoritative owner of the development server, port `3000`, browser automation, database, schema, migrations, dependency installation, manifests, lockfiles, full build, full end-to-end suite, full validation, generated assets, Git integration, and final `Codex_Chats` / `Development_Docs` synchronization. Workers must not imply ownership of resources assigned elsewhere.\n+\n+Every launch update must state that the coordinator retains the original user requirements, decomposition, dependency tracking, conflict prevention, major decisions, output review, integration, contradiction resolution, final validation, final response, and repository synchronization. The coordinator remains accountable for correctness and completion.\n+\n+### Meaningful status changes and later waves\n+\n+Provide a visible update when worker topology, ownership, permissions, objective, dependencies, findings, or integration state changes materially. Meaningful triggers include:\n+\n+- worker launched or a new wave began;\n+- worker scope or ownership materially changed;\n+- ownership transferred;\n+- worker became blocked or failed;\n+- worker reported a major finding;\n+- worker completed;\n+- worker was replaced or cancelled as redundant; or\n+- integration, coordinator review, or independent verification began or completed.\n+\n+If a lane's ownership, objective, permissions, dependencies, or end goal changes, announce the previous scope, new scope, reason, ownership-conflict check, effect on other lanes, and whether the plan was updated **before** allowing work to continue under the revised scope. Workers must never silently broaden their own scope.\n+\n+Newly discovered lanes and later waves require a new manifest with the same information as the initial launch. Label it clearly, such as `Subagent wave 2 started`, `Additional verification lanes started`, or `Replacement lane started`; an earlier manifest never covers workers created later.\n+\n+### Completion, blocking, failure, replacement, and cancellation\n+\n+When a worker finishes, promptly provide a concise status update containing its lane ID and role, completion status, deliverable received, major finding or authorized change, validation performed, unresolved uncertainty, review and integration state, and confirmation that its worker slot was released. Group results received at approximately the same time into a compact table. Distinguish each state explicitly:\n+\n+- **worker complete** means the lane returned its deliverable;\n+- **output reviewed** means the coordinator inspected that deliverable;\n+- **output integrated** means accepted changes or findings were incorporated into the combined result; and\n+- **output independently verified** means a separate check confirmed the material result.\n+\n+A worker's completion report alone is not coordinator verification. A grouped completion report may use fields equivalent to:\n+\n+| Lane | Result   | Key output                   | Validation                       | Uncertainty | Review / integration / verification                      | Slot     |\n+| ---- | -------- | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | -------- |\n+| R1   | Complete | 24 active routes inventoried | Cross-checked against route tree | None        | Reviewed; awaiting synthesis; not independently verified | Released |\n+\n+When a lane becomes blocked or fails, report its exact blocker, preserved partial work, released ownership or resource locks, impact on dependent lanes, whether unrelated lanes continue, recovery action, and whether the lane will be retried, narrowed, split, reassigned, serialized, replaced, or cancelled. Do not hide failure because other lanes succeeded.\n+\n+When replacing a worker, report the prior lane's status and preserved output, why replacement is necessary, the replacement lane's complete launch manifest, ownership handoff, affected dependencies, and confirmation that no material result was lost or left unreviewed.\n+\n+When cancelling a redundant or unnecessary lane, report its lane ID, why it became redundant, what useful output was preserved, which lane now owns any remaining responsibility, which locks or slots were released, and confirmation that no unreviewed material result was discarded.\n+\n+### Grouping and anti-spam safeguard\n+\n+Transparency must remain useful rather than noisy. Group workers launched or completed at approximately the same time into one structured update. Do not require or send updates for every tool call, file opened, search performed, minor internal observation, unchanged status poll, or passage of a few minutes. Do not repeat `still working` messages without a meaningful change, dump full worker prompts unless the user requests them, or expose raw internal reasoning or private chain-of-thought. Announce only meaningful changes in topology, ownership, permissions, status, material findings, dependencies, or integration state.\n+\n+### Final parallel-execution summary\n+\n+For every substantial task that used subagents, the final response must concisely and readably summarize:\n+\n+- total unique subagents used, maximum simultaneous workers, and worker waves;\n+- roles and ownership boundaries, including which lanes were read-only and which modified files;\n+- important results from each lane;\n+- failed, blocked, replaced, or cancelled lanes;\n+- how worker outputs were reviewed, reconciled, integrated, and verified;\n+- whether parallel execution increased capacity, depth, coverage, verification, or reduced elapsed time;\n+- any avoidable duplication or coordination overhead; and\n+- useful improvements for the next task decomposition.\n+\n+Do not reproduce raw worker prompts or private reasoning. Keep the assessment evidence-based, and do not invent numerical percentages when timing or work data was not recorded.\n+\n ## Mandatory parallelization assessment\n*** End Patch";
const r = await tools.apply_patch(patch);
text(r);

Check `gh --version`. If missing, ask the user to install `gh` and stop.\n- Require authenticated `gh` session. Run `gh auth status`. If not authenticated, ask the user to run `gh auth login` (and re-run `gh auth status`) before continuing.\n- Require a local git repository with a clean understanding of which changes belong in the PR.\n\n## Naming conventions\n\n- Branch: `agent/{description}` when starting from main/master/default.\n- Commit: `{description}` (terse).\n- PR title: `{description}` summarizing the full diff.\n\n## Workflow\n\n1. Confirm intended scope.\n   - Run `git status -sb` and inspect the diff before staging.\n   - If the working tree contains unrelated changes, do not default to `git add -A`. Ask the user which files belong in the PR.\n2. Determine the branch strategy.\n   - If on `main`, `master`, or another default branch, create `agent/{description}`.\n   - Otherwise stay on the current branch.\n3. Stage only the intended changes.\n   - Prefer explicit file paths when the worktree is mixed.\n   - Use `git add -A` only when the user has confirmed the whole worktree belongs in scope.\n4. Commit tersely with the confirmed description.\n5. Run the most relevant checks available if they have not already been run.\n   - If checks fail due to missing dependencies or tools, install what is needed and rerun once.\n6. Push with tracking: `git push -u origin $(git branch --show-current)`.\n7. Open a draft PR.\n   - Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for PR creation after the push succeeds.\n   - Derive `repository_full_name` from the remote, for example by normalizing `git remote get-url origin` or by using `gh repo view --json nameWithOwner`.\n   - Derive `head_branch` from `git branch --show-current`.\n   - Derive `base_branch` from the user request when specified; otherwise use the remote default branch, for example via `gh repo view --json defaultBranchRef`.\n   - If the branch is being pushed from a fork or the PR target differs from the remote that was just pushed, prefer `gh pr create` fallback because the connector PR creation flow expects one repository target and may not encode cross-repo head semantics cleanly.\n   - If connector-based PR creation cannot infer the repository or branch cleanly, fall back to `gh pr create --draft --fill --head $(git branch --show-current)`.\n   - Write the PR body to a temp file with real newlines when using CLI fallback so the markdown renders cleanly.\n8. Summarize the result with branch name, commit, PR target, validation, and anything the user still needs to confirm.\n\n## Write Safety\n\n- Never stage unrelated user changes silently.\n- Never push without confirming scope when the worktree is mixed.\n- Default to a draft PR unless the user explicitly asks for a ready-for-review PR.\n- If the repository does not appear to be connected to an accessible GitHub remote, stop and explain the blocker before making assumptions.\n\n## PR Body Expectations\n\nThe PR description should use real Markdown prose and cover:\n\n- what changed\n- why it changed\n- the user or developer impact\n- the root cause when the PR is a fix\n- the checks used to validate it\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

### Additional verification lane starting

**V1 — Governance disclosure verifier**

- **Status:** Starting
- **Mode:** Read-only
- **Owns:** Requirement-by-requirement review of `AGENTS.md`, `PLANS.md`, and both `Codex_Governing` mirrors; scoped diffs and mirror equality only
- **Objective / end goal:** Independently confirm launch visibility, single/multi-worker coverage, later waves, scope transfers, lifecycle failures/cancellations, grouped anti-spam updates, one-writer/exclusive-resource preservation, coordinator accountability, and final reporting
- **Why this lane exists:** Worker self-validation is not coordinator or independent verification
- **Must not modify or control:** Any file, application code, Git state, server, port, browser, database, schema, dependencies, build, tests, or synchronization
- **Deliverable:** Actionable mismatches first, full validation checklist, preservation assessment, mirror-equality result, uncertainty, and confidence
- **Completion criteria:** Every user validation item is mapped to concrete text or reported as a gap
- **Dependencies:** Canonical review and mirror synchronization are complete
- **Integration:** Coordinator resolves any finding before staging; otherwise marks A1/P1 output independently verified
- **Expected benefit:** Independent coverage and regression detection before GitHub publication

One worker is launching; **12 direct-worker slots remain available**. No lanes are blocked or queued, and no further workers are planned unless V1 identifies a material gap. The coordinator retains requirements, conflict resolution, final validation, the path-scoped Git commit/push, final response, and repository synchronization. Git integration and final synchronization remain coordinator-only; all runtime resources remain unused and centralized.

A1 exclusively edited `AGENTS.md`; P1 exclusively edited `PLANS.md`; V1 read-only verified all four governance files. No workers failed, blocked, were replaced, or were cancelled. The coordinator reviewed and reconciled both drafts, updated mirrors, ran final validation, and published the scoped commit. Parallel drafting improved capacity and reduced serial elapsed work; V1 added independent coverage with no material duplication.

## User
**Timestamp:** 2026-07-18T19:18:59.561Z

Strengthen the canonical repository-root `AGENTS.md` and `PLANS.md` so that reducing elapsed completion time is explicitly established as one of the framework’s highest operational priorities.

Preserve all existing requirements for correctness, user intent, evidence, safety, accessibility, security, performance, ownership, testing, integration, disclosure, and final verification.

Do not modify application code or unrelated files.

## Governing priority

Add a prominent statement near the beginning of `AGENTS.md` equivalent to:

> Speed is a first-class operational priority. Codex must organize and execute work to minimize total elapsed completion time while preserving correctness, user intent, safety, required evidence, ownership discipline, and validation quality.

Clarify that speed is not merely a hoped-for result of parallelization. It is an explicit planning, scheduling, delegation, model-routing, and integration objective.

The governing optimization target is:

1. Correct and complete results
2. Minimum practical elapsed completion time
3. Maximum useful capacity, depth, coverage, and verification within that time
4. Minimum avoidable duplication, waiting, ceremony, and coordination overhead

State that correctness and safety remain hard constraints, but within those constraints Codex must choose the execution approach expected to finish sooner.

## Critical-path minimization

Require every Tier 1 and Tier 2 plan to identify the task’s critical path.

The coordinator must:

* identify which work directly controls final completion time;
* launch critical-path work as early as safely possible;
* move prerequisite discovery off the critical path through parallel read-only lanes;
* prevent noncritical work from blocking critical work;
* prioritize uncertain or high-risk dependencies early;
* begin integration incrementally when safe rather than waiting unnecessarily for every unrelated lane;
* immediately start newly unblocked work;
* reassign or split slow lanes when doing so would reduce completion time without losing useful work;
* avoid leaving completed worker output waiting unnecessarily for review or integration;
* prevent the coordinator itself from becoming the critical-path bottleneck.

When multiple valid decompositions exist, prefer the one expected to produce a correct integrated result sooner.

## No unnecessary waiting

Add explicit prohibitions against:

* processing independent workstreams sequentially;
* leaving useful worker slots idle while ready work exists;
* waiting for an entire worker wave when one slot becomes available;
* waiting for all discovery lanes before starting implementation that already has sufficient verified inputs;
* waiting for all implementation lanes before integrating completed independent work when incremental integration is safe;
* requiring every worker to finish before reviewing completed high-priority outputs;
* rerunning expensive global checks after every small lane instead of using focused checks and one integrated gate;
* making every worker rediscover repository structure;
* delaying safe read-only fan-out until every planning field is complete;
* writing oversized plans or status reports whose coordination cost exceeds their practical value;
* performing delegable work in the coordinator merely because delegation requires a briefing;
* maintaining redundant lanes after sufficient evidence exists.

## Fast-start requirement

For every nontrivial task:

1. Complete the minimum safety and repository preflight.
2. Identify immediately ready independent lanes.
3. Publish the required subagent manifest.
4. Launch those lanes without unnecessary delay.
5. Continue refining the dependency graph and detailed plan while safe read-only work proceeds.
6. Launch write lanes as soon as ownership, requirements, and prerequisites are sufficiently established.
7. Keep capacity filled with the highest-value ready work.

The coordinator must not spend a large initial portion of the task only planning when useful bounded investigation can already proceed safely.

## Time-aware lane selection

Extend the lane-value check to include:

* Will this lane reduce total elapsed completion time?
* Does it remove work from the critical path?
* Can it execute simultaneously with existing lanes?
* Will its expected time savings exceed briefing, monitoring, and integration overhead?
* Would combining it with another lane be faster?
* Would splitting a slow or broad lane shorten the critical path?
* Is the worker capability appropriate for the lane’s urgency and complexity?
* Can the result be reviewed and integrated promptly?

A lane should not be created merely because parallel capacity exists. It should normally improve speed, capacity, quality, coverage, verification, or risk reduction enough to justify its overhead.

## Worker-slot utilization

State that:

* meaningful ready worker capacity should normally remain fully utilized;
* the coordinator should maintain a queue of ready and next-ready lanes for substantial tasks;
* newly available slots should be filled immediately with the highest-value ready lane;
* capacity may intentionally remain unused only when no useful independent work exists or when conflict, dependency, safety, or resource constraints prevent additional work;
* the coordinator must briefly record the reason when substantial ready work exists but available capacity is intentionally unused.

Do not require twelve workers when fewer meaningful lanes exist.

Do require the full useful lane count when enough real, non-overlapping work exists.

## Worker capability and reasoning speed

Strengthen model and reasoning routing so that:

* mechanical inventories, repository searches, classifications, repetitive comparisons, and bounded read-only scans use the fastest capable worker configuration available;
* high reasoning effort is reserved for tasks that actually require it;
* the coordinator does not assign maximum reasoning effort to every worker by habit;
* stronger workers are used for difficult architecture, diagnosis, implementation, reconciliation, and verification;
* work may be reassigned from an unnecessarily slow configuration when a faster capable worker can complete it reliably;
* model selection must optimize both reliability and elapsed time.

Do not hardcode model names that are not verified in the installed client.

## Incremental result use

Require the coordinator to use worker output as it becomes available.

The coordinator must:

* review critical results promptly;
* begin synthesis or implementation from sufficiently verified completed lanes;
* avoid waiting for unrelated workers when their results do not block progress;
* surface major findings early;
* preserve unresolved dependencies explicitly;
* integrate in safe increments;
* keep unrelated lanes running during integration.

A completed worker result must not sit unused merely because other lanes are still active.

## Validation speed

Preserve full validation quality while reducing duplicated validation time.

Require:

* focused checks in each implementation lane;
* parallel test shards when safe;
* one owner for expensive integrated checks;
* no complete test suite per worker;
* no duplicate builds without a demonstrated reason;
* early testing of high-risk assumptions;
* final integrated validation only when the state is ready enough to make the result useful;
* reuse of valid test, server, browser, and repository evidence;
* immediate classification of failures so unrelated work can continue.

Speed may never be obtained by silently skipping required validation.

## Communication must not delay execution

Preserve visible subagent disclosure, but clarify that communication should occur immediately before or after launch and must not become a blocking administrative process.

Require:

* compact grouped manifests;
* concise meaningful status changes;
* no duplicate narration;
* no full worker-prompt dumps unless requested;
* no repeated progress messages without new information;
* no delay in starting safe workers merely to produce elaborate chat formatting.

Transparency supports execution; it must not materially slow it.

## PLANS.md additions

Add an explicit `Elapsed-time strategy` section for Tier 1 and Tier 2 plans containing fields equivalent to:

```text
Critical path:
Earliest safe fan-out point:
Ready lanes at launch:
Highest-risk dependency to resolve early:
Expected serialized bottlenecks:
Incremental integration opportunities:
Expensive shared operations:
Worker-slot utilization strategy:
Conditions for splitting or reassigning a slow lane:
Conditions allowing work to proceed before all lanes finish:
```

Add time-aware fields to each relevant lane:

```text
Critical-path relationship:
Earliest safe start:
Expected blocking dependencies:
Elapsed-time benefit:
Can integration begin before other lanes finish?
Reassignment or split trigger:
```

Do not require speculative duration estimates or invented percentages.

## Final execution assessment

For substantial tasks, require the final response to assess concisely:

* whether independent work began promptly;
* whether useful capacity remained unnecessarily idle;
* whether critical-path work received priority;
* whether completed results were integrated promptly;
* whether parallelization materially reduced elapsed time;
* what avoidable waiting, duplication, or planning overhead occurred;
* how a similar future task could finish faster.

Keep this evidence-based. Do not invent timing figures that were not measured.

## Final governing statement

Include a concise principle equivalent to:

> Codex must move as quickly as possible without becoming careless. Parallelization, specialization, progressive planning, early risk resolution, incremental integration, focused validation, and prompt worker reuse exist to deliver a correct and complete result in the least practical elapsed time.

After updating the canonical files:

* synchronize `Codex_Governing/Agents.md`;
* synchronize `Codex_Governing/Plans.md`;
* verify byte-for-byte mirror equality;
* run scoped Markdown checks;
* run `git diff --check`;
* confirm no application or unrelated files changed;
* report exactly how the framework now prioritizes speed without weakening quality.

The development-only `/dev/animations` route is not an obsolete application.\n- Generated, local, archived, or excluded locations include `node_modules`, `.next`, `out`, `build`, `coverage`, `.runtime`, `.data`, `backups`, local database files, `test-results`, `playwright-report`, `playwright/.auth`, `artifacts/screenshots`, `artifacts/validation`, local logs, and Codex import/cache/temp content identified by `.gitignore`. Instruction-looking files inside ignored validation snapshots, other worktrees, archives, or mirrors do not apply to the current checkout unless their own repository root/current-directory scope makes them applicable.\n\nVerify the canonical implementation before broad exploration or modification. Do not create port-specific application copies, abandoned local variants, duplicate runtimes, or parallel implementations of existing canonical surfaces.\n\n### Verified commands and ports\n\n| Purpose                           | Verified command or behavior                       |\n| --------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |\n| Canonical development start       | `npm run dev:full`                                 |\n| Canonical development stop        | `npm run dev:stop`                                 |\n| Canonical development address     | `http://127.0.0.1:3000`; port `3000` has one owner |\n| Next-only development after setup | `npm run dev`                                      |\n| Production build                  | `npm run build`                                    |\n| Lint                              | `npm run lint`                                     |\n| Format check                      | `npm run format:check`                             |\n| Strict type check                 | `npm run typecheck`                                |\n| Unit/component tests              | `npm test`                                         |\n| End-to-end tests                  | `npm run test:e2e`                                 |\n| Animation asset contracts         | `npm run assets:validate`                          |\n| Full repository validation        | `npm run validate`                                 |\n\nThere is no separate verified `test:integration` script; integration behavior is covered by the focused Vitest/Playwright checks and the full `npm run validate` gate. Playwright normally owns an isolated server on port `3100`, and full validation normally uses port `3200` for production restart proof. Those are controlled validation resources, not authorization to start competing development servers. An explicitly isolated alternate development port is an exception for a demonstrated need, not a new application copy.\n\n## Common parallelization triggers\n\nThe following commonly satisfy the mandatory two-lane threshold:\n\n- multiple packages, services, subsystems, routes, pages, feature domains, or acceptance criteria;\n- repository-wide audits, large codebase discovery, independent bug hypotheses, or multiple evidence sources;\n- multiple animation libraries, renderers, or independent review categories;\n- separable frontend, backend, database, API, deployment, documentation, test, accessibility, security, performance, functional, or maintainability concerns;\n- broad refactors or implementations with non-overlapping ownership boundaries;\n- independent test suites or validation surfaces; or\n- noisy exploration that would displace requirements and integration state from the coordinator's context.\n\nBroad read-heavy work strongly favors parallel specialist lanes.\n\n## Work that normally remains serialized\n\nKeep work sequential when delegation overhead exceeds the work or when ordering or exclusive ownership is material, including:\n\n- tiny localized tasks;\n- strictly dependent steps;\n- changes concentrated in the same small group of files;\n- ordered migration chains;\n- package-manager, manifest, or lockfile updates with one authoritative writer;\n- shared schema, configuration, entrypoint, route registry, global style, or central provider changes;\n- destructive operations, production deployment, final integration, and final merges;\n- one exclusive runtime, browser, database, or deployment resource; or\n- any situation where parallel writes would create a race or ambiguous ownership.\n\nFor a substantial task performed primarily sequentially, record the concrete dependency, safety, resource, or conflict reason. Coordinator convenience alone is not sufficient.\n\n## Coordinator responsibilities\n\nThe main agent is the coordinator. It must:\n\n- retain the complete user request and governing constraints;\n- perform or own repository/Git preflight, decompose the dependency graph, assign non-overlapping scopes, and prevent duplicate work;\n- give every lane a bounded objective, authorization, ownership boundary, evidence requirement, validation requirement, completion condition, and return format;\n- track dependencies, lane progress, available capacity, and shared resources while keeping raw logs and noisy exploration out of the main context;\n- start replacement or newly ready lanes as soon as safe capacity becomes available;\n- collect concise evidence-backed summaries, reconcile disagreements, and directly verify severe, risky, or uncertain findings;\n- own shared-file integration, integrate in dependency order, review the combined diff, run final cross-cutting validation, and reread the original request before completion;\n- produce one coherent final result rather than concatenating worker reports; and\n- own the single mandatory `Codex_Chats` / `Development_Docs` finalization workflow unless it explicitly delegates that exclusive responsibility.\n\nThe coordinator's primary role is orchestration, requirements retention, decision-making, conflict resolution, integration, and final verification. It must not remain a general-purpose worker while safe meaningful work remains undelegated. Do not redo delegated work except for necessary verification. Workers must not fetch, pull, rebase, merge, commit, push, or run the synchronization workflow unless the coordinator explicitly grants that bounded authority.\n\n## Subagent contract\n\nEvery delegated lane must state:\n\n- lane ID, role, precise objective, relevant background, and governing requirements;\n- explicit scope and non-scope;\n- read-only or write authorization;\n- owned files, directories, components, or concerns, plus forbidden paths and resources;\n- dependencies, prerequisite evidence, and shared resources it may not modify;\n- required searches, output, evidence, focused validation, and confidence level;\n- concise return schema covering what was inspected and found, paths/symbols, evidence, authorized changes, tests, failures, uncertainty, conflicts, recommendations, and completion status;\n- completion and stop conditions; and\n- instructions to report blockers, overlaps, partial results, uncertainty, and ownership conflicts honestly.\n\nSubagents must not silently broaden scope. They return distilled findings or implementation summaries with relevant paths, symbols, commands, reproduction evidence, and confidence, not unfiltered command logs.\n\n## Capacity and wave scheduling\n\n- Treat the configured thread cap as an upper bound, not proof that every client currently exposes that many slots. Reserve the root thread for coordination when it counts toward the cap.\n- Launch every ready independent lane up to actual safe capacity. Queue excess lanes by dependency, criticality, uncertainty, and value.\n- When a slot opens, start the next ready lane immediately; do not wait for the rest of a wave.\n- Reuse coordinator discovery and a shared repository census. Bound each lane to its own evidence or ownership area instead of making every worker rediscover the entire repository.\n- Avoid redundant searches, duplicated expensive operations, idle workers, and artificial lanes created only to raise agent count.\n- Add lanes dynamically when new independent work appears. Collapse or cancel redundant lanes, preserve useful partial results, and never ignore a slower worker's material output merely because faster lanes finished first.\n\n## Worker capability and reasoning routing\n\nWhen the installed client supports per-agent selection, verify available models and reasoning settings rather than inventing aliases. Use the fastest capable available worker, generally with balanced reasoning, for mechanical repository census, file/symbol/route/import inventories, dependency mapping, repetitive documentation checks, straightforward result classification, and homogeneous read-only audits. Use a stronger worker with high reasoning for complex code paths, performance, accessibility, security, architecture, data flow, difficult implementation, subtle regressions, runtime diagnosis, and cross-system contracts. Use the strongest appropriate available capability for coordination, decomposition, reconciliation, high-risk decisions, shared architecture, integration, synthesis, and final verification. Never downgrade a lane when doing so would materially reduce reliability, and do not run every mechanical lane at the coordinator's maximum reasoning effort.\n\n## Read-heavy workflow policy\n\nAudits, reviews, discovery, research, mapping, inventories, diagnostics, and verification strongly default to parallel read-only specialist lanes divided by subsystem, concern, library, package, route group, risk category, evidence source, or acceptance criterion. Use repository-wide search before deep file inspection, reuse a shared repository census, and give every lane a distinct evidence boundary. A verification lane or the coordinator must recheck severe or disputed findings. Reports distinguish confirmed findings, probable findings, and unverified suspicions and include paths, symbols, reproduction steps, evidence, and confidence where applicable.\n\nRead-only lanes may inspect a shared file concurrently but may not mutate Git state, runtime state, databases, generated output, or external systems without explicit authorization.\n\nBroad read-heavy tasks should normally use more workers than write-heavy tasks because read-only concurrency presents fewer collision risks.\n\n## Write-heavy workflow policy\n\n- Partition implementation by non-overlapping files, components, packages, or services. Exactly one agent may write a file at a time.\n- Use separate Codex worktrees for independent implementation chats or lanes when supported and appropriate. Otherwise declare strict path ownership before editing.\n- Designate one integration owner for shared files. Lockfiles, manifests, central configuration, schemas, migrations, generated artifacts, global styles, providers, route definitions, and entrypoints each have one authoritative lane.\n- Sequence dependent changes through the plan's dependency graph. Use an explicit handoff before ownership moves between agents.\n- Each implementation lane runs focused checks for its bounded work; centralized integration validation follows combination.\n- Never casually merge over, overwrite, stage, or discard another lane's or the user's changes.\n\n## Runtime and expensive-resource ownership\n\nAssign one authoritative owner for each shared or expensive resource: the development server, port `3000`, shared browser sessions, databases, migration execution, dependency installation, manifests and lockfiles, production builds, full end-to-end suites, full validation, generated assets, deployment environments, final Git integration, `Codex_Chats` synchronization, and `Development_Docs` synchronization.\n\nOther lanes may consume existing logs or results and request targeted checks from the owner. They must not start a competing server, mutate shared database state, regenerate shared artifacts, install dependencies, or run a duplicate full suite without a demonstrated need and safe isolation. Before acquiring a runtime, inspect recorded state and listeners; release or hand off ownership explicitly.\n\n## Testing and validation parallelization\n\n- Run safe independent unit-test groups concurrently and shard by package, subsystem, feature, or test type.\n- Let implementation lanes run focused checks for their work. Do not make every lane run the complete suite.\n- Assign one integrated type-check owner, one integrated lint owner when needed, one production-build owner, one final E2E owner, and one final full-validation owner. A single validation lane may own several of these serialized operations.\n- Run the full required acceptance gate once on the ready integrated state unless the task or failure investigation requires an additional run.\n- Preserve failure evidence and classify failures as pre-existing, task-induced, environmental, intermittent, or unresolved.\n- Treat shared-database mutation suites and runtime-dependent checks as exclusive resources unless isolation is proven.\n\n## Context management\n\nThe main thread retains requirements, decisions, dependency state, ownership, integration status, unresolved questions, and final conclusions. Subagents absorb noisy searches, logs, stack traces, and repetitive evidence gathering, then return compact structured summaries. Store or reference large raw output instead of repeatedly pasting it. Summarization must not drop user requirements, unknowns, decisions, blockers, or unreviewed results.\n\n## Failure and recovery\n\nIf one lane fails, independent lanes continue. The failed lane returns partial evidence and its blocker and safely releases any file, runtime, or resource lock. Preserve completed work and useful output. Retry only when conditions change; otherwise narrow, split, reassign, or serialize only the affected portion. Do not restart all work because one lane failed. Record downstream impact, disclose unresolved gaps, and request user intervention only when authority, information, or an external state change is genuinely required.\n\n## Completion standard\n\nDo not claim a nontrivial task complete until:\n\n- every required workstream is complete or explicitly accounted for;\n- all agent output that could materially change the result has been reviewed;\n- duplicates, contradictions, and conflicting conclusions are reconciled or disclosed;\n- shared-file integration and ownership handoffs are complete;\n- focused and required cross-cutting validation has run on the integrated state;\n- the result has been rechecked against the original request;\n- skipped checks, known limitations, unresolved risks, and pre-existing failures are reported accurately;\n- final repository synchronization has run exactly once under the authorized coordinator; and\n- no worker remains active whose output could materially change the conclusion.\n\n## Execution plans\n\nEvery task uses the Tier 0 parallelization minimum in root `PLANS.md`. Standard multi-step work uses Tier 1, and every substantial cross-system, audit, refactor, migration, documentation, architecture, long-running, or high-risk implementation uses Tier 2. The plan may remain in task coordination state or be written to the requested artifact. When a durable authored plan is required, place it under `Development_Docs`; root `PLANS.md` is the reusable standard, not a competing plan archive. Update plans when discoveries change dependencies, ownership, safe concurrency, validation, or risk, while preserving completed work.\n\n## Shared development documentation\n\n`Development_Docs` at the repository root is the canonical shared location for development requirements, project history, architecture, decisions, implementation plans, and related authored documentation.\n\nBefore beginning work that depends on those materials:\n\n1. Confirm the repository, active branch, upstream, and complete working-tree state.\n2. Preserve any local modifications, then run `git fetch --prune origin`.\n3. Determine whether the branch is behind, ahead, or diverged with `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\n4. Pull with `--ff-only`, or rebase when the repository's branch policy permits it, only when the operation is demonstrably safe. Never discard local work to synchronize.\n5. If the same document changed on two computers, preserve both sets of work and surface conflicts. Perform a normal textual merge only when clearly correct. Keep clearly differentiated variants of unmergeable binary documents when appropriate, and never push unresolved conflict markers.\n\nNever use `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, `git checkout -- .`, `git restore .`, `git push --force`, or `git push --force-with-lease` as synchronization shortcuts.\n\nInspect `Development_Docs` before work when project history, requirements, architecture, decisions, or plans are relevant. Update a relevant document when an implementation decision makes it inaccurate, but do not modify unrelated documents merely because they were read. Preserve authored content and native formatting, avoid normalization churn, and do not use the directory for routine terminal output or generated test/build artifacts. Never upload secrets, credentials, private configuration, production data, or unapproved sensitive company information.\n\n## Mandatory task finalization\n\nFor every Codex task performed in this repository, conversation and development-document synchronization is a required finalization gate. Do not delete or weaken these steps:\n\n1. Complete and test the requested project work.\n2. Inspect the complete working tree and classify task changes, `Development_Docs` changes, `Codex_Chats` changes, unrelated pre-existing work, and generated files. Never stage the whole repository by default.\n3. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --dry-run`. Review conversation classifications and the structured `development_docs` status, including eligible, excluded, suspicious, large, conflicted, renamed, and deleted paths.\n4. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py` to ingest every currently accessible project-associated Codex session and any newer export in `Codex_Chats/imports/`, and to commit eligible `Development_Docs` changes through the same path-scoped workflow.\n5. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --validate`, inspect the exact synchronization-owned Git diff, and re-check the complete repository status.\n6. Confirm each command's exit status and structured report. Never claim a conversation was archived unless a source was read and a transcript was written successfully. Never claim documentation was synchronized unless the reported commit and push state prove it.\n7. If synchronization content changed, confirm the scoped commit and permitted push succeeded and verify the remote SHA. If nothing changed, confirm no empty commit was created. Report unrelated remaining changes accurately.\n8. Include concise `Chat archive:` and `Development docs:` result lines in the final response.\n\nThe synchronizer is deliberately not a `post-commit` hook because many prompts finish without a code commit. The Codex host currently exposes no repository task-completion hook that can be versioned here, so this instruction is authoritative. A task's final assistant message can only be captured on the next synchronization because it does not exist until after the pre-finalization run; the next task must update that same stable conversation rather than creating a duplicate.\n\n`Development_Docs` remains a normal Git-tracked directory. Do not copy it into `Codex_Chats`, archive it as transcripts, or maintain a duplicate mirror. The synchronizer may commit only explicit eligible paths; it must leave ignored, suspicious, oversized, conflicted, and unrelated files untouched. Do not commit raw ChatGPT exports or bypass a secret warning, unsafe Git state, ambiguous classification, source failure, integrity failure, branch divergence, or protected-branch policy.\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

Queue excess lanes by dependency, criticality, uncertainty, and value.\r\n- When a slot opens, start the next ready lane immediately; do not wait for the rest of a wave.\r\n- Reuse coordinator discovery and a shared repository census. Bound each lane to its own evidence or ownership area instead of making every worker rediscover the entire repository.\r\n- Avoid redundant searches, duplicated expensive operations, idle workers, and artificial lanes created only to raise agent count.\r\n- Add lanes dynamically when new independent work appears. Collapse or cancel redundant lanes, preserve useful partial results, and never ignore a slower worker's material output merely because faster lanes finished first.\r\n\r\n## Worker capability and reasoning routing\r\n\r\nWhen the installed client supports per-agent selection, verify available models and reasoning settings rather than inventing aliases. Use the fastest capable available worker, generally with balanced reasoning, for mechanical repository census, file/symbol/route/import inventories, dependency mapping, repetitive documentation checks, straightforward result classification, and homogeneous read-only audits. Use a stronger worker with high reasoning for complex code paths, performance, accessibility, security, architecture, data flow, difficult implementation, subtle regressions, runtime diagnosis, and cross-system contracts. Use the strongest appropriate available capability for coordination, decomposition, reconciliation, high-risk decisions, shared architecture, integration, synthesis, and final verification. Never downgrade a lane when doing so would materially reduce reliability, and do not run every mechanical lane at the coordinator's maximum reasoning effort.\r\n\r\n## Read-heavy workflow policy\r\n\r\nAudits, reviews, discovery, research, mapping, inventories, diagnostics, and verification strongly default to parallel read-only specialist lanes divided by subsystem, concern, library, package, route group, risk category, evidence source, or acceptance criterion. Use repository-wide search before deep file inspection, reuse a shared repository census, and give every lane a distinct evidence boundary. A verification lane or the coordinator must recheck severe or disputed findings. Reports distinguish confirmed findings, probable findings, and unverified suspicions and include paths, symbols, reproduction steps, evidence, and confidence where applicable.\r\n\r\nRead-only lanes may inspect a shared file concurrently but may not mutate Git state, runtime state, databases, generated output, or external systems without explicit authorization.\r\n\r\nBroad read-heavy tasks should normally use more workers than write-heavy tasks because read-only concurrency presents fewer collision risks.\r\n\r\n## Write-heavy workflow policy\r\n\r\n- Partition implementation by non-overlapping files, components, packages, or services. Exactly one agent may write a file at a time.\r\n- Use separate Codex worktrees for independent implementation chats or lanes when supported and appropriate. Otherwise declare strict path ownership before editing.\r\n- Designate one integration owner for shared files. Lockfiles, manifests, central configuration, schemas, migrations, generated artifacts, global styles, providers, route definitions, and entrypoints each have one authoritative lane.\r\n- Sequence dependent changes through the plan's dependency graph. Use an explicit handoff before ownership moves between agents.\r\n- Each implementation lane runs focused checks for its bounded work; centralized integration validation follows combination.\r\n- Never casually merge over, overwrite, stage, or discard another lane's or the user's changes.\r\n\r\n## Runtime and expensive-resource ownership\r\n\r\nAssign one authoritative owner for each shared or expensive resource: the development server, port `3000`, shared browser sessions, databases, migration execution, dependency installation, manifests and lockfiles, production builds, full end-to-end suites, full validation, generated assets, deployment environments, final Git integration, `Codex_Chats` synchronization, and `Development_Docs` synchronization.\r\n\r\nOther lanes may consume existing logs or results and request targeted checks from the owner. They must not start a competing server, mutate shared database state, regenerate shared artifacts, install dependencies, or run a duplicate full suite without a demonstrated need and safe isolation. Before acquiring a runtime, inspect recorded state and listeners; release or hand off ownership explicitly.\r\n\r\n## Testing and validation parallelization\r\n\r\n- Run safe independent unit-test groups concurrently and shard by package, subsystem, feature, or test type.\r\n- Let implementation lanes run focused checks for their work. Do not make every lane run the complete suite.\r\n- Assign one integrated type-check owner, one integrated lint owner when needed, one production-build owner, one final E2E owner, and one final full-validation owner. A single validation lane may own several of these serialized operations.\r\n- Run the full required acceptance gate once on the ready integrated state unless the task or failure investigation requires an additional run.\r\n- Preserve failure evidence and classify failures as pre-existing, task-induced, environmental, intermittent, or unresolved.\r\n- Treat shared-database mutation suites and runtime-dependent checks as exclusive resources unless isolation is proven.\r\n\r\n## Context management\r\n\r\nThe main thread retains requirements, decisions, dependency state, ownership, integration status, unresolved questions, and final conclusions. Subagents absorb noisy searches, logs, stack traces, and repetitive evidence gathering, then return compact structured summaries. Store or reference large raw output instead of repeatedly pasting it. Summarization must not drop user requirements, unknowns, decisions, blockers, or unreviewed results.\r\n\r\n## Failure and recovery\r\n\r\nIf one lane fails, independent lanes continue. The failed lane returns partial evidence and its blocker and safely releases any file, runtime, or resource lock. Preserve completed work and useful output. Retry only when conditions change; otherwise narrow, split, reassign, or serialize only the affected portion. Do not restart all work because one lane failed. Record downstream impact, disclose unresolved gaps, and request user intervention only when authority, information, or an external state change is genuinely required.\r\n\r\n## Completion standard\r\n\r\nDo not claim a nontrivial task complete until:\r\n\r\n- every required workstream is complete or explicitly accounted for;\r\n- all agent output that could materially change the result has been reviewed;\r\n- duplicates, contradictions, and conflicting conclusions are reconciled or disclosed;\r\n- shared-file integration and ownership handoffs are complete;\r\n- focused and required cross-cutting validation has run on the integrated state;\r\n- the result has been rechecked against the original request;\r\n- skipped checks, known limitations, unresolved risks, and pre-existing failures are reported accurately;\r\n- final repository synchronization has run exactly once under the authorized coordinator; and\r\n- no worker remains active whose output could materially change the conclusion.\r\n\r\n## Execution plans\r\n\r\nEvery task uses the Tier 0 parallelization minimum in root `PLANS.md`. Standard multi-step work uses Tier 1, and every substantial cross-system, audit, refactor, migration, documentation, architecture, long-running, or high-risk implementation uses Tier 2. The plan may remain in task coordination state or be written to the requested artifact. When a durable authored plan is required, place it under `Development_Docs`; root `PLANS.md` is the reusable standard, not a competing plan archive. Update plans when discoveries change dependencies, ownership, safe concurrency, validation, or risk, while preserving completed work.\r\n\r\n## Shared development documentation\r\n\r\n`Development_Docs` at the repository root is the canonical shared location for development requirements, project history, architecture, decisions, implementation plans, and related authored documentation.\r\n\r\nBefore beginning work that depends on those materials:\r\n\r\n1. Confirm the repository, active branch, upstream, and complete working-tree state.\r\n2. Preserve any local modifications, then run `git fetch --prune origin`.\r\n3. Determine whether the branch is behind, ahead, or diverged with `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\r\n4. Pull with `--ff-only`, or rebase when the repository's branch policy permits it, only when the operation is demonstrably safe. Never discard local work to synchronize.\r\n5. If the same document changed on two computers, preserve both sets of work and surface conflicts. Perform a normal textual merge only when clearly correct. Keep clearly differentiated variants of unmergeable binary documents when appropriate, and never push unresolved conflict markers.\r\n\r\nNever use `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, `git checkout -- .`, `git restore .`, `git push --force`, or `git push --force-with-lease` as synchronization shortcuts.\r\n\r\nInspect `Development_Docs` before work when project history, requirements, architecture, decisions, or plans are relevant. Update a relevant document when an implementation decision makes it inaccurate, but do not modify unrelated documents merely because they were read. Preserve authored content and native formatting, avoid normalization churn, and do not use the directory for routine terminal output or generated test/build artifacts. Never upload secrets, credentials, private configuration, production data, or unapproved sensitive company information.\r\n\r\n## Mandatory task finalization\r\n\r\nFor every Codex task performed in this repository, conversation and development-document synchronization is a required finalization gate. Do not delete or weaken these steps:\r\n\r\n1. Complete and test the requested project work.\r\n2. Inspect the complete working tree and classify task changes, `Development_Docs` changes, `Codex_Chats` changes, unrelated pre-existing work, and generated files. Never stage the whole repository by default.\r\n3. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --dry-run`. Review conversation classifications and the structured `development_docs` status, including eligible, excluded, suspicious, large, conflicted, renamed, and deleted paths.\r\n4. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py` to ingest every currently accessible project-associated Codex session and any newer export in `Codex_Chats/imports/`, and to commit eligible `Development_Docs` changes through the same path-scoped workflow.\r\n5. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --validate`, inspect the exact synchronization-owned Git diff, and re-check the complete repository status.\r\n6. Confirm each command's exit status and structured report. Never claim a conversation was archived unless a source was read and a transcript was written successfully. Never claim documentation was synchronized unless the reported commit and push state prove it.\r\n7. If synchronization content changed, confirm the scoped commit and permitted push succeeded and verify the remote SHA. If nothing changed, confirm no empty commit was created. Report unrelated remaining changes accurately.\r\n8. Include concise `Chat archive:` and `Development docs:` result lines in the final response.\r\n\r\nThe synchronizer is deliberately not a `post-commit` hook because many prompts finish without a code commit. The Codex host currently exposes no repository task-completion hook that can be versioned here, so this instruction is authoritative. A task's final assistant message can only be captured on the next synchronization because it does not exist until after the pre-finalization run; the next task must update that same stable conversation rather than creating a duplicate.\r\n\r\n`Development_Docs` remains a normal Git-tracked directory. Do not copy it into `Codex_Chats`, archive it as transcripts, or maintain a duplicate mirror. The synchronizer may commit only explicit eligible paths; it must leave ignored, suspicious, oversized, conflicted, and unrelated files untouched. Do not commit raw ChatGPT exports or bypass a secret warning, unsafe Git state, ambiguous classification, source failure, integrity failure, branch divergence, or protected-branch policy.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

[{"text": "Script completed\nWall time 0.4 seconds\nOutput:\n", "type": "input_text"}, {"text": "Exit code: 0\nWall time: 0.4 seconds\nOutput:\n# Repository execution-planning standard\r\n\r\nThis document defines how Codex plans and coordinates work in this repository. It is a reusable execution standard, not a plan for one feature or audit. Root `AGENTS.md` supplies the governing behavior, requires this file at the thresholds below, and remains the automatically discovered repository instruction source. Current user instructions and governing product documents continue to define scope and desired results.\r\n\r\nRoot `PLANS.md` does not replace `Development_Docs`. When a task requires a durable authored plan or implementation record, store that task-specific artifact under `Development_Docs` in accordance with its rules. A plan may otherwise remain in the task's coordination state or in the user-requested artifact.\r\n\r\n## Progressive planning tiers\r\n\r\nEvery task gets a parallelization assessment, but planning ceremony scales with task size and risk.\r\n\r\n### Tier 0: localized task\r\n\r\nUse Tier 0 only when the task is genuinely small, scope is narrow, one file or a tightly related file group is involved, no meaningful independent workstream exists, no shared runtime/database/migration/lockfile/deployment risk exists, and delegation would cost more than it saves.\r\n\r\nTier 0 records only the objective, affected scope, quick parallelization assessment, focused validation, and finalization. A distinct verification lane may still be useful.\r\n\r\n### Tier 1: standard multi-step task\r\n\r\nUse Tier 1 for medium work. Record the objective; scope and exclusions; governing sources; repository/working-tree reality; assumptions and unknowns; compact dependency graph and parallelization assessment; lane matrix; subagent communication plan; file and shared-resource ownership; focused testing; integration; and final verification. For each proposed lane, record its expected capacity, quality or coverage, elapsed-time, independence, and coordinator-integration value as defined below. The lane matrix must include the user-visible worker identity, announcement, ownership, lifecycle, deliverable, benefit, and integration fields defined below. Use two to five workers when that many meaningful lanes exist.\r\n\r\n### Tier 2: large or high-risk task\r\n\r\nUse the complete template in this file for repository-wide audits, large implementations, major refactors, multiple feature surfaces, database changes, migrations, deployment, high-risk security work, architecture changes, long-running or cross-computer work, tasks with six or more meaningful lanes, or work with substantial context/coordination-loss risk. Record the expected capacity, quality or coverage, elapsed-time, independence, and coordinator-integration value of every proposed lane. The complete lane matrix and subagent communication plan are mandatory whenever workers are used. Target six to twelve direct workers when the dependency graph and installed capacity support that many real lanes.\r\n\r\n## Progressive planning rule\r\n\r\nMinimum safe preflight comes first. Launch obvious independent read-only lanes immediately afterward and refine the detailed plan while they run. Before or immediately after any launch, disclose the worker manifest in chat as required by the subagent communication plan; planning refinement never makes a launched worker invisible. No write lane begins before file/resource ownership and integration boundaries are defined. Plans are living coordination artifacts, not paperwork gates: make them detailed enough to protect quality without delaying safe useful work. \"Complete every planning table before starting any worker\" is prohibited when ready read-only lanes exist.\r\n\r\n## Planning principles\r\n\r\nAn execution plan must:\r\n\r\n- be self-contained enough for another coordinator to continue without rediscovering the task;\r\n- preserve the exact user goal, terminology, constraints, acceptance criteria, methodology, and output format;\r\n- separate requirements and invariants from implementation choices;\r\n- identify verified facts, assumptions, unknowns, and canonical sources;\r\n- represent work as a dependency graph rather than a falsely linear checklist;\r\n- maximize safe, useful concurrency while identifying conflict-prone and serialized work;\r\n- establish one coordinator, clear lane ownership, and one writer per file at a time;\r\n- identify shared runtime and expensive resources with authoritative owners;\r\n- define evidence, focused validation, integration validation, and completion criteria before implementation;\r\n- remain updateable as discoveries change scope, dependencies, ownership, risk, or validation;\r\n- make every subagent launch and material lifecycle change visible to the user through concise structured chat updates;\r\n- record significant decisions and discoveries without exposing private chain-of-thought; and\r\n- preserve completed work when the plan changes.\r\n\r\n## Capacity and wave scheduling\r\n\r\nRecord:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nConfigured open-thread capacity:\r\nCoordinator reservation:\r\nMaximum normal direct workers:\r\nCurrent ready lanes:\r\nCurrently active lanes:\r\nBlocked lanes:\r\nAvailable slots:\r\nNext ready lanes:\r\nExclusive resources currently held:\r\n```\r\n\r\nKeep safe capacity filled with ready meaningful work, reserve the coordinator when the client counts the root session, queue excess work by dependency and value, and fill newly available slots immediately. Do not wait for an entire wave to finish. Avoid idle or artificial workers, stop or collapse redundant work, and preserve results between waves.\r\n\r\n## Lane-decomposition quality check\r\n\r\nBefore spawning a worker, confirm:\r\n\r\n- the lane has a distinct result and evidence or ownership boundary;\r\n- it can proceed without another lane's unfinished result;\r\n- it can run without conflicting writes;\r\n- any exclusive resource and its owner are explicit;\r\n- its output materially helps the final result and does not duplicate another lane;\r\n- the selected worker is capable of completing it independently;\r\n- the return format is clear; and\r\n- the coordinator can integrate the result.\r\n\r\nMerge or serialize lanes that fail this check.\r\n\r\nApply this lane-value check to every proposed Tier 1 and Tier 2 lane:\r\n\r\n- Does this lane increase useful work capacity?\r\n- Does it add distinct expertise, evidence, implementation, testing, or verification?\r\n- Can it reduce the task's critical-path duration?\r\n- Is its expected value greater than its delegation and integration overhead?\r\n- Can its output be integrated without creating conflicts or duplicated work?\r\n\r\nA lane should normally be created when it provides at least one strong benefit in capacity, quality, coverage, verification, or elapsed time and does not create disproportionate conflict or coordination cost. A lane that fails this value check should be revised, merged, serialized, or omitted rather than used ceremonially.\r\n\r\n## Complete Tier 2 execution-plan template\r\n\r\nTier 2 uses every section below. Tier 1 uses the identified compact subset; Tier 0 uses its stated minimum. \"Not applicable\" is acceptable only with a short reason.\r\n\r\n### 1. Task identity\r\n\r\n```text\r\nTask title:\r\nDate:\r\nCoordinator:\r\nRepository and root:\r\nCurrent branch/worktree and commit:\r\nGoverning documents:\r\nRelated issue, phase, audit, or feature:\r\nCurrent task instructions:\r\nPlan location/status:\r\n```\r\n\r\nCopy or faithfully summarize the controlling task instructions. Link to the full source when a summary could lose detail.\r\n\r\n### 2. Objective\r\n\r\nRecord:\r\n\r\n- desired end state;\r\n- user-visible outcome;\r\n- technical outcome; and\r\n- why the work is being done.\r\n\r\nUse outcome language. Do not describe activity alone as success.\r\n\r\n### 3. Scope\r\n\r\nRecord:\r\n\r\n- included work;\r\n- explicit exclusions;\r\n- areas and behaviors that must remain unchanged;\r\n- existing completed or uncommitted work that must be preserved; and\r\n- actions that require separate user authority.\r\n\r\nParallelization never broadens this scope.\r\n\r\n### 4. Sources of truth\r\n\r\nList, in priority order:\r\n\r\n- current user instructions;\r\n- governing specifications and approved `Development_Docs` material;\r\n- maintained architecture, security, testing, and operational documentation;\r\n- canonical code and data directories;\r\n- relevant tests and runtime evidence;\r\n- external dependencies or authoritative external documentation; and\r\n- the rule to apply when sources disagree.\r\n\r\nState which facts were verified in the current task and which were inherited or require confirmation.\r\n\r\n### 5. Repository reality check\r\n\r\nBefore delegation or modification, capture:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nGit repository root:\r\nCanonical remote/repository:\r\nActive application:\r\nActive branch and HEAD:\r\nUpstream and ahead/behind/diverged state:\r\nComplete working-tree classification:\r\nCanonical startup command:\r\nRequired application port:\r\nRelevant focused and full validation commands:\r\nGenerated, archived, obsolete, or excluded trees:\r\nExisting uncommitted work and owner/provenance if known:\r\nSafe synchronization action taken or deferred:\r\n```\r\n\r\nFor this repository, verify rather than assume the current baseline. The expected canonical shape is one root Next.js application on `main`, normal startup through `npm run dev:full`, and normal development port `3000`. If reality differs, stop treating the expectation as fact and update the plan.\r\n\r\nDo not inspect secrets, overwrite local databases, or treat generated validation copies as source. Classify unrelated and pre-existing work before editing.\r\n\r\n### 6. Assumptions and unknowns\r\n\r\nUse this table:\r\n\r\n| ID  | Assumption or unknown | Impact if wrong | Verification method | Responsible lane | Can work proceed first? | Status/evidence |\r\n| --- | --------------------- | --------------- | ------------------- | ---------------- | ----------------------- | --------------- |\r\n| A1  |                       |                 |                     |                  | Yes/No                  |                 |\r\n\r\nDo not silently convert an unknown into a requirement or conclusion.\r\n\r\n### 7. Parallelization assessment\r\n\r\nAnswer explicitly:\r\n\r\n- Which work is meaningfully independent?\r\n- Which work depends on another result?\r\n- Which lanes can begin immediately?\r\n- Which steps must be serialized, and for what concrete reason?\r\n- Which resources are shared or exclusive?\r\n- Which files or interfaces are conflict-prone?\r\n- Which work should be read-only?\r\n- Which write lanes need isolated worktrees?\r\n- What is the maximum safe and useful concurrency under current capacity?\r\n- Why does the chosen decomposition improve speed, coverage, reliability, or context management without redundant work?\r\n- What conditions would cause a lane to be split, collapsed, cancelled, or serialized?\r\n\r\nFor every proposed Tier 1 and Tier 2 lane, record the following in the assessment, lane matrix, or an adjacent lane record:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nCapacity benefit:\r\nQuality or coverage benefit:\r\nElapsed-time benefit:\r\nWhy this lane should run independently:\r\nExpected coordinator integration cost:\r\n```\r\n\r\nThese entries must state the expected task benefit and likely coordination cost concretely enough to support the lane-value check. They may be concise, but they must distinguish meaningful capacity, specialization, evidence, implementation, testing, verification, or critical-path value from artificial worker count.\r\n\r\nFor a substantial task that remains primarily sequential, document the dependency, safety, exclusive-resource, or overlapping-file reason. Ease of coordination is not enough.\r\n\r\n### 8. Dependency graph\r\n\r\nRepresent lane identifiers, prerequisites, fan-out, fan-in, the critical path, and failure independence. A compact text or Mermaid graph is acceptable. For example:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nP0 repository preflight\r\n|-- R1 read-only subsystem inventory --\\\r\n|-- R2 read-only test/risk inventory ----+-- I1 coordinator reconciliation\r\n`-- R3 read-only requirements review ---/\r\n                                         |-- W1 isolated implementation lane --\\\r\n                                         `-- W2 isolated implementation lane --+-- I2 shared-file integration\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]
